How to Draw (Opt): Draw the array on the left and a 2-column table (Key=Num, Value=Index) on the right. Draw a line from the current array element to the table looking for its complement.
Draw L1 on top, L2 below it. Draw vertical boxes circling the nodes at index 'i'. Write a "+ Carry" bubble at the top of the next column.
Draw the string. Draw a bracket `[` for the left pointer and `]` for the right. Erase and redraw `]` moving right. Erase and redraw `[` moving right when a collision occurs.
Draw Array A on top of Array B. Draw a red vertical partition line through both. Write the boundary conditions (L1 <= R2 and L2 <= R1) explicitly next to the line.
Draw the string. Place an "X" on one character. Draw outward-facing arrows `<--` and `-->` originating from the "X" moving step-by-step.
Write the string out linearly. Draw long arcs jumping over characters for the top row. Draw secondary, shorter alternating arcs for the middle rows.
Draw two boxes: `X` and `Rev`. Draw a loop where the rightmost digit of `X` falls off, slides over, and attaches to the right side of `Rev`.
Draw a timeline. Place a pointer moving left to right. Draw bubbles above it representing the current state transitioning: [Start] -> [Sign] -> [Digit Accumulation] -> [Done].
Draw a large number. Draw a scissor line in the middle. Show the right half flipping over and comparing itself to the left half.
Draw a grid. String `s` on the left (Y axis), pattern `p` on top (X axis). Draw arrows showing that the current cell's True/False value is pulled from the cell above, left, or top-left.
Draw the array. Place an 'L' arrow at index 0 and 'R' at N-1. Draw a water block between them. Cross out the shorter line and draw its arrow stepping inward.
Draw two parallel lists: Values [1000, 900, 500...] and Symbols [M, CM, D...]. Point at the largest value <= num. Subtract value, append symbol.
Write "MCMXCIV". Write the integer values below each letter. Draw an arrow from C(100) to M(1000). Since 100 < 1000, write a minus sign.
Draw a `prefix` box initially holding String[0]. Draw scissors chopping off the rightmost letter of the `prefix` box until `String[i].startsWith(prefix)` is true.
Draw a sorted array. Stick a thumbtack on 'i'. Bracket the rest of the array. Put 'L' at the start of the bracket, 'R' at the end. Move L/R based on the sum.
Draw a sorted array. Pin 'i'. Place L and R. Write the `target`. Draw arrows pulling L or R inward trying to get the sum magnetically closer to the target value.

Problem Title: Letter Combinations of a Phone Number
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a root node. Branch out to 'a', 'b', 'c'. From 'a', branch to 'd', 'e', 'f'. Trace a line down to "ad", then back up to 'a', then down to "ae".
Draw a sorted array. Stick a red thumbtack on 'i' and a blue one on 'j'. Bracket the rest. Put L at j+1, R at the end. Move L/R to hit the target.

Problem Title: Remove Nth Node From End of List
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a linked list. Place 'Fast' 2 nodes ahead of 'Slow'. Draw a rigid stick connecting them. Slide both right until 'Fast' falls off the end. 'Slow' bypasses the next node.
Draw a vertical bucket. Push `(`, `[`, `{` down into it. When `}` arrives, check the top of the bucket. Since it's `{`, cross them both out.
Draw List 1 and List 2. Draw a star (Dummy) with an arrow `current`. Point `current` to the smaller of P1/P2. Cross out the old pointer and move it forward.
Draw a root node. Branch left for '(' and right for ')'. Put a lock icon on the right branch if `open == close` (meaning you can't add a closing bracket yet).
Draw a funnel (Heap) holding K items. Drop the smallest item out the bottom. Immediately replace it with the next item from the list it came from.
Draw `prev -> A -> B -> next`. Draw curved arrows overriding the straight ones: `prev` arcs to `B`, `B` arcs backward to `A`, `A` arcs to `next`.
Draw a list. Put a bracket around K nodes. Reverse the arrows inside the bracket. Draw the `prevGroupTail` pointing to the new head of this bracket.
Draw an array. Place `S` (Slow) and `F` (Fast) at index 1. Slide `F` right until `arr[F] != arr[F-1]`. Draw a curved arrow from `F` overwriting the value at `S`, then step `S` forward.
Draw the array. Put `S` and `F` at index 0. If `arr[F] == val`, move `F` right. If `arr[F] != val`, draw an arrow copying `arr[F]` to `arr[S]`, then move both right.

Problem Title: Find the Index of the First Occurrence in a String
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the haystack and needle. When a mismatch hits at index `j`, look up `LPS[j-1]`. Draw a long sweeping arrow shifting the needle forward, bypassing already matched prefixes.

Draw the Dividend block. Draw a Divisor block that visually doubles in size (1x, 2x, 4x, 8x) until it almost overflows the Dividend. Subtract the largest chunk, write down the multiplier, and restart.

Problem Title: Substring with Concatenation of All Words
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the main string. Bracket a window of size `W * len`. Inside the bracket, draw vertical lines slicing it into individual words. Compare those slices to a drawn Hash Map of target words.

Draw the array as a 2D line graph. Scanning left from the end, draw an arrow pointing to the first drop in elevation (the dip). Swap it with a slightly higher peak to its right, then draw a flat line showing the rest being sorted (reversed).
Draw the string. Draw two tally boxes (L and R). Scan forward. When L and R match, draw a "Max Length" box updating. If R exceeds L, draw a red "X" and zero out the tallies. Repeat backwards.
Draw a line graph that goes up, breaks, drops down, and goes up again. Draw a vertical line down the middle. Circle the half that is an unbroken straight line and write "Is Target Here?"

Problem Title: Find First and Last Position of Element in Sorted Array
How to Draw (Opt): Draw an array. Draw one binary search tree converging to place a `[` on the leftmost target. Draw a completely separate binary search tree converging to place a `]` on the rightmost target.
Draw a sorted array. Draw `L` and `R` pointers closing in. Draw the moment they cross: `R` is now behind `L`. Draw an arrow pointing at `L` saying "Insert Here".
Draw one cell at `(r, c)`. Draw three distinct buckets (Row, Col, Box) next to it. Drop the number into all three. If a bucket already has that number, draw a red "X" (Invalid).
Draw a mini 3x3 grid. Write a '2'. Draw an arrow to the next cell, write '5'. Hit a wall. Erase '5', write '6'. Erase '6', write '7'. Draw the "erase and rewrite" motion heavily.
Draw "111221". Put 'S' and 'E' at index 0. Slide 'E' right until it hits '2'. Bracket the "111". Write "Three 1s" -> append "31". Move 'S' to 'E'.
Draw a target number (e.g., 7). Draw a fork. Left: pick '2' (Target becomes 5, stay on '2'). Right: skip '2' (Target remains 7, move to '3').
Draw a sorted array: `[1, 1, 2, 5, 6]`. Start a branch with the first '1'. When the loop moves to the second '1', draw a giant red curved arrow jumping over it to '2'.
Draw an array. Point at number '3' in index 0. Draw a curved arrow picking up '3' and swapping it with whatever is at index 2. Repeat until index 0 holds '1' or a useless number (like -5).
Draw a bar chart. Put L on the left, R on the right. If L's max is shorter than R's max, draw a blue water block above L, and move L inward. Erase and redraw the max line as they climb.

Draw standard elementary school vertical multiplication. Draw arrows linking the top digit `i` and bottom digit `j` directly into a pre-sized array box at position `i+j+1` (holding the digit) and `i+j` (holding the carry).

Draw string S and pattern P. Draw a star icon over the `*` in P. Draw a pointer moving right in S. When a mismatch hits, draw a curved arrow snapping the pointer back to the star icon, shifting it right by one.
Draw the array. Bracket index 0. That's Jump 0. Look at the values inside the bracket to find the furthest reach. Bracket that new range. That's Jump 1. Repeat until the bracket covers the end.

Draw an array `[1, 2, 3]`. Point at index 0. Swap `1` with itself, branch down. Swap `1` with `2`, branch down. Swap `1` with `3`, branch down. Always draw a curved arrow swapping them back when moving up.
Draw a sorted array `[1, 1, 2]`. Branch off the first `1`. When the loop reaches the second `1`, draw a thick red "X" through its branch, writing "Duplicate Sibling Skipped".

Draw a square grid. Draw a diagonal line from top-left to bottom-right. Fold the numbers across the diagonal (Transpose). Then, draw a vertical line down the middle and flip the left and right sides (Reverse).
Draw three buckets. Label one "aet", one "ant", one "abt". Draw strings ("eat", "tea", "ate") falling cleanly into the "aet" bucket.
Draw a starting box `2^10`. Next arrow points to `4^5` (base squared, power halved). Next points to `4 * 16^2` (handled the odd power). Next points to `4 * 256^1`.
Draw an N×N board. Place a 'Q'. Draw vertical and diagonal red lines emanating from it. Move to the next row and only place a 'Q' in the white (unshaded) spaces.
Draw a simple "counter" box next to a backtracking tree. Every time a "Leaf" node is reached (success), show the counter incrementing `+1`. No board is drawn.
Draw a running total box. As you move through the array, if the box becomes negative, "empty" it (reset to 0) and start fresh with the next number.
Draw a grid. Draw four thick lines representing the borders. After each side is traversed, draw an arrow showing that border line physically moving inward.
Draw the array. Draw a flag representing the `farthest` reachable point. As you walk forward, push the flag as far as the current spot allows. If you ever walk past the flag, you lose.
Draw a horizontal timeline. Place intervals as segments above it. Draw a "Merge" bracket joining segments that physically touch or overlap.
Draw three shaded regions on a timeline: "Left (No Overlap)", "Middle (Merging Zone)", and "Right (Remaining)". Place the intervals into their respective regions.
Draw the string with an arrow at the very end pointing left. Draw the arrow hopping over empty spaces, then dragging through the letters of the last word.
Draw a square grid. Draw four arrows (T, B, L, R) pointing inward. As you finish a side, physically move that arrow's line one step toward the center.
Draw a set of digits {1, 2, 3, 4}. Use the formula to "pluck" one number out and place it in the result. Repeat with the remaining smaller set.
Draw the list. Draw a curved line connecting the last node back to the first. Then, draw a "scissor" icon cutting the link at the specific index to turn the circle back into a line.
Draw an M x N grid. Fill the first row and column with 1s. For cell `(1,1)`, look at the 1 above and 1 to the left; write '2' in the cell. Continue summing neighbors.
Draw the grid with a blacked-out obstacle cell. When filling the grid, if you reach the black cell, write '0' and move on. Show how the sums "flow" around the obstacle.
Draw the grid. For each cell, look at the values above and to the left. Pick the smaller one, add the current cell's value, and write it over the original number.
Draw 6-8 circles (States). Draw arrows between them labeled with types (Digit, '.', 'e', '+/-'). Trace the string "3.14e-10" as a marble rolling from circle to circle.
Draw the array `[9, 9, 9]`. Start at the end. Change 9 to 0, move left. Change 9 to 0, move left. Change 9 to 0. Draw a "New Box" at the front and put '1' inside.
Stack "1010" and "1011". Draw vertical columns. 0+1=1, carry 0. 1+1=0, carry 1. 0+0+1=1, carry 0. 1+1=0, carry 1. Result: "10101".

Optimised Pattern: Greedy Word Packing + Round Robin Spacing
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a line with 3 words. Space needed = 7. Gaps = 2. 7 / 2 = 3 spaces each, with 1 remainder. Give 4 spaces to gap 1 and 3 spaces to gap 2.
Draw a line from 0 to 100. Mark 50. 50^2 = 2500 (Too big). Cut range to [0, 49]. Mark 25. 25^2 = 625 (Too big). Repeat until range collapses.
Draw steps 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Under step 1, write '1'. Under step 2, write '2'. For step 3, add 1+2=3. For step 4, add 2+3=5.
Draw a vertical stack. For "/home/user/../docs/./", push "home", push "user". Encounter "..", pop "user". Encounter "docs", push "docs". Result: "/home/docs".
Draw a grid with "horse" on top and "ros" on left. Fill cell by cell. Show how each cell "looks" at its 3 neighbors to the top and left to find the cheapest path.
Draw a grid. When you hit a 0 at `(2,3)`, draw an arrow pointing to the "0" at the start of Row 2 and the "0" at the top of Col 3. Those are your "flags".
Draw a grid. Label cells from 0 to 11. Pick `mid = 5`. Draw the math: 5 / 4 = Row  1, 5 % 4 = Col  1. Target the cell `(1,1)` and split the virtual array.
Draw an array. Place `L` at the start, `H` at the end, `M` in the middle. Show 0s being kicked to the left of `L` and 2s being kicked to the right of `H`.
Draw string S and a frequency map for T. As `R` moves, subtract from map. If a map value hits 0, a character is "satisfied." When all are satisfied, move `L` to shrink.
Draw a tree starting at root. Branch to 1, 2, 3... From 1, branch only to 2, 3... (skipping 1). From 2, branch only to 3... (skipping 1, 2).
Draw a 2D grid. Trace a path A to B to C. At C, if there's no match, "undo" the path C to B to A to try a different direction from B.

Problem Title: Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array II
How to Draw (Opt): Draw an array. Place `S` at index 2, `F` at index 2. Compare `arr[F]` with `arr[S-2]`. If different, write `arr[F]` to `arr[S]` and move `S`.

Problem Title: Search in Rotated Sorted Array II
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a graph with two plateaus at the same height. Show that if the middle is also that height, we must "step in" from both sides until we find a slope to apply binary search.

Problem Title: Remove Duplicates from Sorted List II
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a list `1->2->2->3`. Use a `Dummy`. `prev` stays at 1. When it sees `2->2`, it jumps directly to `3`. Draw the pointer bypassing the entire "2" block.
Draw `1->1->1->2`. Pointer stays at the first `1`. It deletes the second `1`, then the third `1`. Finally, it moves to `2`.
Draw a stack. Push heights: `[2, 1, 5, 6]`. When `2` comes, it's smaller than the previous height? No. When `1` comes, it pops the taller ones, calculates area, and takes their place.
Draw a grid. For row 1, heights are `[1, 0, 1]`. For row 2, if there's a 1 below a 1, height becomes 2. Now run the "Histogram" visual on these heights.
Draw the original list. Draw two stars (Dummies). As you move through the list, "sever" the arrow of the current node and point it to the tail of the appropriate dummy list.
Draw a 3D grid where `dp[len][i][j]` represents if `s1[i...i+len]` is a scramble of `s2[j...j+len]`. Fill small lengths first and build up to length N.
Draw `nums1` and `nums2`. Place `p` at the far right of `nums1`. Compare `p1` and `p2`. Whichever is bigger moves to `p`, then `p` and that pointer move left.
Draw a list: `0, 1`. Mirror it: `1, 0`. Add 2^1 (which is 2) to the mirrored part: `2, 3`. New list: `0, 1, 3, 2`.
Draw a tree for `[1, 2, 2]`. From the root, branch to 1 and the *first* 2. Put a red "X" through the branch for the *second* 2, saying "Already picked a 2 here."
Draw a line of cells. For each cell, look back at the previous one and the one before that. Draw arrows showing the "flow" of counts, but only if the decoding rules allow it.
Draw `pre -> curr -> next -> ...`. Draw a "jump" arrow where `curr` skips `next` to point to `next.next`, and `next` is "re-threaded" to point to where `pre.next` was.
Draw a tree with a maximum depth of 4. From the root, branch 1, 2, or 3 digits. If you branch "300", put a red "X" through it because it's > 255.
Draw a tree and a vertical bucket (Stack). Push the left spine of the tree into the bucket. Pop one, then look at its right child and repeat.
Draw a Map where the Key is the range (e.g., "1-3") and the Value is a list of root pointers. Show the algorithm "checking out" a tree from the library instead of building it.
Draw an array `dp` from 0 to N. Fill `dp[0]=1, dp[1]=1`. For `dp[3]`, show three pairs of multiplications: (dp[0] * dp[2]) + (dp[1] * dp[1]) + (dp[2] * dp[0]).
Draw a grid with s1 on top and s2 on left. Start at (0,0) as `T`. A cell is `T` if (left is `T` AND s1 matches s3) OR (top is `T` AND s2 matches s3).
Draw a tree. At the root, write (-infty, infty). For the left child, write (-infty, RootValue). For the right, (RootValue, infty). Keep narrowing the boxes.
Draw a tree. Draw a "thread" (dashed line) from the right-most node of a left subtree back to the current root. Use this thread to return without a stack.
Draw two identical trees side-by-side. Point to the root of both simultaneously. Move both "fingers" to the left child, then right child, checking for a match at every stop.

Optimised Pattern: Two-pointer recursive DFS (or BFS with queue).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a single tree. Use two different colored pens (e.g., red and blue) to trace the mirrored paths simultaneously from the root downward.
Draw the tree. Draw horizontal dashed lines separating each level. Draw a box beside it representing the queue updating at each dashed line.

Problem Title: Binary Tree Zigzag Level Order Traversal
Optimised Pattern: BFS with a Double-ended Queue (Deque) and a boolean direction flag.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the tree. Draw solid arrows moving left-to-right on row 1, right-to-left on row 2, alternating down.
Write '1' next to all leaf nodes. Draw arrows pointing up to parents, crossing out the parent and writing the max(children) + 1 .

Problem Title: Construct Binary Tree from Preorder and Inorder Traversal
Optimised Pattern: Hash Map for O(1) inorder index lookup + Recursive DFS.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the Hash Map explicitly. Cross off preorder elements sequentially, drawing them immediately as tree nodes.

Problem Title: Construct Binary Tree from Inorder and Postorder Traversal
Optimised Pattern: Hash Map for O(1) inorder index lookup + Recursive DFS (Process postorder backwards: Root, Right, Left).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the Hash Map. Cross off postorder elements from right-to-left. Draw the tree building downwards, strictly branching right before left.

Problem Title: Binary Tree Level Order Traversal II
Optimised Pattern: BFS with a Queue + Prepending to a Deque/Linked List for the result (or standard array reversal, which is practically optimal in many languages).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the tree. As you process a level, draw its resulting array box underneath the previously processed level's array box, pushing older ones up.

Problem Title: Convert Sorted Array to Binary Search Tree
Optimised Pattern: Divide and Conquer / DFS. Find mid, make root, recurse left half, recurse right half.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the array. Circle the middle element. Draw arrows from the left half to a left child placeholder, and right half to a right child placeholder. Repeat.

Problem Title: Convert Sorted List to Binary Search Tree
Optimised Pattern: Inorder Simulation (DFS). Calculate list length. Build left subtree, assign current list node as root, advance list pointer, build right subtree.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the Linked List. Draw the empty tree structure. Trace the Inorder traversal path, filling in the empty tree nodes strictly in the order of the Linked List.

Optimised Pattern: Bottom-Up DFS. Return the height of the subtree if it is balanced. If it is ever unbalanced, return -1 immediately to short-circuit.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the tree. Write heights next to nodes starting from the bottom. If a parent sees a difference > 1, write "-1" boldly and strike through the rest of the path up.

Optimised Pattern: BFS (Level Order Traversal) with an early exit on the first leaf node encountered.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw horizontal lines for levels. Stop drawing and circle the very first node you see that lacks children. Cross out the rest of the tree below it.

Optimised Pattern: DFS (Pre-order) with target subtraction (passing `target - node.val` down the recursion).
How to Draw (Opt): Write the starting target at the root. Cross it out, write `target - root.val`, and draw an arrow passing this new number down to children.

Optimised Pattern: DFS with Backtracking. Maintain a single shared path list: `push` node, recurse, `pop` node.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a single list box. Write a value in it (push), draw a downward arrow. Erase the value (pop), draw an upward arrow. Repeat.

Problem Title: Flatten Binary Tree to Linked List
Optimised Pattern: Reverse Post-order Traversal (Right, Left, Root) maintaining a global `prev` pointer.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the tree. Start at the bottom-right-most node. Draw an arrow from its `right` pointer to `prev` (initially null). Update `prev` to point to it. Move backwards.
Draw a 2D matrix. `s` on rows, `t` on cols. Fill base cases (empty `t` = 1). Fill row by row, carrying values from the top and top-left cells.

Problem Title: Populating Next Right Pointers in Each Node
Optimised Pattern: Level-by-Level Linked List Traversal (using existing `next` pointers to traverse horizontally).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a tree. Use your finger to trace horizontally across level L. As your finger moves, draw straight dashed lines connecting the children on level L+1.

Problem Title: Populating Next Right Pointers in Each Node II
Optimised Pattern: Linked List Traversal with a Dummy Head (tracking the start of the next level).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the tree. Draw a "Dummy" circle on the left of an empty level. As you scan the level above, draw arrows from the dummy chain to the found children.

Optimised Pattern: Dynamic Programming (Tabulation). Building the current row iteratively from the directly previous row.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a staggered grid. Fill the edges with 1s. To fill an empty cell, draw two V-shaped arrows coming from the top-left and top-right cells, adding them together.

Optimised Pattern: 1D Dynamic Programming (Rolling Array) calculated strictly from right-to-left.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a single 1D array. Update the values strictly from right to left using the old left value and the current value. Show how left-to-right overwrites data prematurely.

Optimised Pattern: Bottom-Up Dynamic Programming (In-place or rolling 1D array).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the triangle. Start at the bottom row. Move up one row. For each cell, look at the two cells directly below it, pick the smaller, and add it to the current cell. Cross out the bottom row.

Problem Title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a line graph of prices. As you move left to right, keep a dotted horizontal line at the lowest point seen so far. Measure the vertical gap to the current point.

Problem Title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock II
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a price graph. Circle every upward-sloping line segment. Draw "+" signs over them and a total sum box at the end.

Problem Title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock III
Optimised Pattern: Dynamic Programming (4-state variables) or Prefix/Suffix Profit Arrays.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a 2xN grid or two 1D arrays (Left-to-Right max profit and Right-to-Left max profit). Sum them up to find the peak.

Optimised Pattern: Bottom-Up DFS (Post-order Traversal) with a Global Max tracker.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a tree. Write the "contribution" value (best path up) next to each node. Use a "Global Max" box in the corner to update as you see bigger combined paths.
Draw the string. Place a 'L' finger on the first letter and a 'R' finger on the last. Move them inward, skipping symbols until they point at letters.

Optimised Pattern: BFS (to find shortest distances) + DFS/Backtracking (to reconstruct paths).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a level-order graph. Label each word with its level (0, 1, 2...). Only draw arrows that move from level k to k+1.

Optimised Pattern: BFS with Character Mutation (a to z).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the start word. Draw 26 small tick marks for the first letter. Circle only the tick marks that match a real word. Repeat.
Draw a number line. Circle the numbers you have. Put a "Start Here" flag only on the left-most number of each clustered island.
Draw the tree. Write the running number *inside* each node as you go down (e.g., root is 1, left child is 12, its child is 123).
Draw the grid. Use a highlighter to "color in" every 'O' that touches the edge, and any 'O' touching those. Any 'O' without color gets turned into an 'X'.
Draw a decision tree. On each arrow, write the substring chosen. If it's a palindrome, draw a green check; if not, draw a red X and stop that branch.
Draw the 1D DP array. For a cell `dp[i]`, draw several "look-back" arrows to previous cells j where a palindrome was found, picking the minimum.

Optimised Pattern: DFS/BFS with a Hash Map (Mapping Original Nodes to Cloned Nodes).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the Original graph on the left and an empty space on the right. Draw a "Map" table in the middle. As you touch an Original node, draw the Clone and update the Map.

Optimised Pattern: Greedy One-Pass (The Total Net Gain Rule).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a line graph of the running fuel balance. Identify the lowest point (the "trough"). Draw a flag on the very next station to show the optimal start.
Draw the ratings. Draw the candy counts after the first pass. Draw a second row of candy counts after the backward pass, taking the max of the two states.
Write numbers in binary. Line them up. Cross out pairs of identical bit columns to show they result in 0. Circle the remaining bits.
Draw a 32-row vertical stack. For each number, put a mark in the rows corresponding to its '1' bits. Write the total count next to each row.
Draw the list. Draw a new node right next to each old node. Draw a "jump" arrow from an old node's random to the target's *adjacent* copy.
Draw the string and a boolean array below it. For a cell dp[i] , draw arrows "looking back" to previous 'True' indices to find matching words.
Draw a "Memo" table. As the recursion completes a branch, write the result list into the table. Draw arrows from future branches directly to that table entry.
Draw a list with a loop. Place an 'S' and an 'F' at the start. Move 'S' one node and 'F' two nodes. Repeat until they occupy the same node.
Draw the loop. Mark the meeting point 'M'. Draw an arrow from 'Head' and 'M' moving at the same speed. Mark the junction where they collide.

Optimised Pattern: Find Middle + Reverse Second Half + Interweave.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the list. Draw a line through the middle. Draw arrows reversing the second half. Then draw zigzag lines connecting nodes from the first and second sections.
Draw the tree. Draw a vertical stack box. Put 'Root' in. Take it out, put its 'Right' then 'Left' in. Repeat. Order in result: 1, 2, 3...

Optimised Pattern: Iterative Traversal using Two Stacks (or One Stack + Reverse).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw two stack boxes. Move items from S1 to S2 in (Root-Right-Left) order. Draw the result of popping S2 to see the (Left-Right-Root) order.
Draw a Map (Key rightarrow Node) and a DLL. When a key is accessed, draw an arrow moving that specific node to the front of the line.

Optimised Pattern: Insertion Sort with Dummy Head and Predecessor Pointers.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a 'Sorted' section and an 'Unsorted' section. Use a dashed line. Move nodes one by one across the line, drawing the new 'next' arrows.

Optimised Pattern: Merge Sort (Bottom-Up Iterative for O(1) space, or Top-Down Recursive).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the list. Draw a tree-like structure splitting it in half, then half again. Draw arrows merging the small pieces into larger sorted chains.

Optimised Pattern: Hash Map for Slopes (per starting point).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a central point. Draw rays coming out of it to other points. Group the rays that have the same angle. Write the count next to each group.
Draw a vertical bin. Drop numbers into it. When you see "+", take the top two out, add them, and drop the result back in.
Write "blue is sky". Reverse the letters: "yks si eulb". Then circle "yks" and flip it to "sky", circle "si" to "is", etc.

Optimised Pattern: Dynamic Programming (Kadane's variation with Min/Max tracking).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the array. Below it, draw two rows: 'Max' and 'Min'. Fill them as you move right. Use a swap arrow whenever you hit a negative number.

Problem Title: Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array
How to Draw (Opt): Draw two sorted lines shifted. Mark 'L', 'M', 'H'. Show how comparing 'M' to 'H' tells you which "slope" the minimum is on.

Problem Title: Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array II
Optimised Pattern: Binary Search with Duplicate Handling (O(log N) average, O(N) worst).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw an array like [2, 2, 2, 0, 2] . Show how L, M, and H are all the same, forcing the 'H' pointer to move just one step left.

Optimised Pattern: Two Stacks (Data Stack + Min Stack) or One Stack with Pairs.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw two parallel stacks. When you drop a number in the left, drop the *lowest seen so far* in the right. Popping keeps them synchronized.
Draw the tree. For each node on the left spine, erase its arrows and draw new ones pointing 'up' to the parent and 'left' to the sibling.

Optimised Pattern: Buffered Chunk Processing with Bound Checking.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a target array and a 4-slot internal buffer. Draw arrows moving characters from buffer to array, stopping precisely at index N-1.

Problem Title: Read N Characters Given Read4 II
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a persistent 4-slot box. Mark where the "last read" ended with a pointer. Show the next call starting from that exact pointer.

Problem Title: Longest Substring with At Most Two Distinct Characters
Optimised Pattern: Sliding Window with Hash Map (Frequency Counter).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a string. Draw a sliding box over it. Below, draw a small table. As the box expands, add letters to the table. If size > 2, shrink the box from the left.
Draw two lists merging. Trace pA through A and then through B. Trace pB through B and then through A. Show them landing on the same node simultaneously.
Draw S and T parallel. Draw a vertical line through matching chars. At the mismatch, draw a box around the remaining parts and link them with an '=' sign.
Draw a line chart. Mark L, R, and M. Draw an uphill arrow based on the comparison of M and M+1 to show the direction of search.
Draw array boxes. Draw arrows between them. Label the "missing" range values directly on the arrows between the boxes.
Draw N-1 buckets. Place the numbers into them. Draw a large arrow between the max of one bucket and the min of the next.
Draw the strings. Draw two pointers. Circle the digits between the dots and write the integer value (e.g., "001" rightarrow 1) above the circle.
Draw the long division. Beside it, keep a "Remainder Log." When you write a remainder, check if it's already in the log. If yes, draw a loop arrow back to its first occurrence.

Problem Title: Two Sum II - Input Array Is Sorted
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the sorted array. Put a finger on the first and last elements. Slide them inward, noting if the sum needs to "grow" or "shrink."
Write the number. Draw a box for "Remainder" and "Quotient." Perform the math, write the letter, and repeat with the new quotient.
Draw the array. Use a "Battle Box." Put the first number in. If the next is same, add a +1. If different, do a -1. If box hits 0, clear and put the next number in.

Problem Title: Two Sum III - Data structure design
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a Map. For `add`, just update the counts. For `find(10)`, iterate through the keys, subtract from 10, and do a quick "Is it in the map?" check.
Draw a single box labeled 'Result'. As you read 'A', 'B', 'C', update the box: 0 to 1, 1 to (1 x 26 + 2) = 28, etc.
Draw a number line up to n. Circle every multiple of 5. For 25, draw two circles (since it's 5^2). Count total circles.
Draw the BST and a vertical stack. Draw an arrow down the left spine. As you pop a node, draw a small arrow to its right child and then down *that* node's left spine.
Draw the grid. Start at the bottom-right corner with '1'. Move left and up, calculating health required based on the cell's damage/heal value.
Draw Table A. Draw Table B. Draw lines connecting matching IDs. If a row in A has no line, draw a box with "NULL" in the B columns.

Optimised Pattern: Subquery with LIMIT 1 OFFSET 1 wrapped in IFNULL .
How to Draw (Opt): Draw Table A. Draw a smaller Table B (filtered for salaries < Max). Circle the highest value in Table B.

Optimised Pattern: Function with Local Variable Offset (N-1) and LIMIT .
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a vertical sorted list. Draw a sliding window box that stops exactly at the N-th position.

Optimised Pattern: Window Function: DENSE_RANK() OVER(ORDER BY Score DESC) .
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the scores. Group identical values with a circle. Assign a rank number to each circle.

Optimised Pattern: Custom Greedy Sorting (Comparator: S1+S2 vs S2+S1).

Draw the table columns. Add two virtual columns labeled "Next" and "Next+1". Fill them and circle rows where all three match.

Problem Title: Employees Earning More Than Their Managers
How to Draw (Opt): Draw two boxes (Employee a, Employee b). Link them with an arrow based on ID. Write their salaries on the arrow and circle if the first is larger.
Draw several buckets labeled with emails. Place dots in buckets. Circle buckets containing more than one dot.

Optimised Pattern: LEFT JOIN where Orders.Id IS NULL (or NOT IN with subquery).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw two tables. Draw connection lines for orders. Circle the customers in the first table that have no outgoing lines.

Optimised Pattern: Join with a GROUP BY subquery or Window Function.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw tables grouped by Dept. Highlight the top row in each. Draw an arrow from these top rows to the final result set.
Draw columns representing departments. Within each, write salaries in order. Mark the first three unique value levels.

Problem Title: Reverse Words in a String II
Optimised Pattern: Double In-place Reversal (Reverse Whole + Reverse Words).
How to Draw (Opt): Write "sky is blue". Step 1: Write "eulb si yks". Step 2: Circle "eulb" and flip to "blue", "si" to "is", "yks" to "sky".

Optimised Pattern: Sliding Window + Hash Set (or Rolling Hash).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the string. Draw a sliding box of width 10. Below it, draw a table. As the box moves, write the content in the table; mark if you write a duplicate.

Problem Title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock IV
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a K x N grid. Fill it row by row. For each cell, look at the cell directly above it and the max value from the row above.
Draw two 32-bit slots. Draw an arrow from the LSB of 'n' to the LSB of 'res'. Show 'res' shifting left and 'n' shifting right.
Write n = 101100. Draw an arrow to the rightmost 1. Erase it (n = 101000). Repeat. Count how many times you erased.

Optimised Pattern: Unix Pipeline ( cat | tr | sort | uniq -c | sort -nr ).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw 5 boxes in a line connected by pipes (|). Label them: Clean rightarrow Sort rightarrow Count rightarrow Rank. Draw data moving through.

Optimised Pattern: Regex Pattern Matching ( grep -P or awk ).

Draw a stack of 20 papers. Draw a single arrow pointing directly to the 10th paper from the top.
Draw two identical lists. Connect identical emails. For each connection, put an 'X' over the row with the bigger number.
Draw the table. Draw a second copy shifted down by one row. Align the rows and circle pairs where the top row temperature is higher.
Draw a row of houses with values. Below, draw a row of "Max Profit" boxes. Fill each box by looking at the two boxes immediately to its left.

Optimised Pattern: Recursive DFS (Root arrow Right arrow Left).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the tree. Trace your pen from the root strictly down the right-most branches first. When you hit a depth you haven't marked, write the number down.
Draw the grid. Use a highlighter to color one island at a time. Each time you finish a "color," add 1 to your total island count.
Draw two 32-bit arrays on top of each other. Box the identical bits on the left. Cross out differing bits on the right. Replace crossed out with 0s.
Draw nodes connected by arrows. If it loops to 1, draw a straight path. If it cycles, draw a literal circle of numbers. Put a "Tortoise" and "Hare" marker on the nodes.
Draw rectangles with [Val|Next] split. Draw curved arrows for pointers. Cross out arrows and draw new ones bypassing the target values.
Draw a 1D tape. Shade out the multiples using different colors or stroke styles (e.g., / for 2s,  for 3s).

Optimised Pattern: Two Hash Maps / Two Frequency Arrays
How to Draw (Opt): Draw two columns of nodes (S characters left, T characters right). Draw directional arrows from left to right. Ensure 1-to-1 mapping.

Optimised Pattern: Iterative In-Place Reversal (Three Pointers: prev, curr, next)
How to Draw (Opt): Draw 4 boxed nodes. Draw arrows pointing right. Use a red pen to cross out the right arrow and draw a left arrow one by one.
Draw circles with directional arrows. Draw a small box next to each circle (in-degree). Erase arrows and decrement boxes to 0.
Draw a root circle. Draw branching lines labeled with letters. Mark end-of-word nodes with a double circle or a star.
Draw an array. Draw a bracket `[ ]` around the elements. Expand the right side `]`, then shrink the left side `[` like an inchworm.

Optimised Pattern: Topological Sort (DFS with 3 States or Kahn's BFS)
How to Draw (Opt): Draw graph nodes. Shade them lightly (visiting). When a path finishes, shade them darkly (visited) and write the node in an array.

Problem Title: Design Add and Search Words Data Structure
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a tree root with letters as edges. Draw a dashed circle enclosing all children of a node when a '.' is encountered to show parallel search paths.

Draw two horizontal arrays. One crosses out the last element, one crosses out the first. Trace the max(curr+prev2, prev1) formula across both.
Write the combined string horizontally. Below it, draw the LPS integer array. Draw arrows jumping back to previous LPS values when characters mismatch.

Problem Title: Kth Largest Element in an Array

Draw a circle representing a Set. Read numbers one by one. Draw an arrow pointing into the circle. If the number is already inside, draw a collision spark.

Optimised Pattern: Sweep Line Algorithm + Max Heap / TreeMap

Draw an array. Draw a bracket `[` and `]` enclosing K elements. Shift both brackets right by 1. Keep a mini-list of what's inside the brackets.
Draw a row of open boxes (buckets). Read a number, map it to a box. Check if the box has an item, or if immediate left/right neighbor boxes have close items.
Draw the original grid. Below it, draw an empty grid. Fill values cell-by-cell. For a '1', look at three neighbors, find min, add 1. Circle the max value.

Optimised Pattern: Binary Search on Leaf Nodes + Tree Height Properties
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a tree. Draw a vertical line down the leftmost and rightmost paths. If paths are equal length, it's a perfect triangle. Use math instead of counting.
Draw two horizontal lines (X-segments). Mark where they overlap. Do the same for vertical lines (Y-segments). The box formed by the overlaps is the intersection.

Optimised Pattern: Stack-Based Single Pass with Sign Tracking
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a ribbon of the expression. Draw a "Stack" bucket. Put a result and sign into the bucket when '(' appears. Pour it back out at ')'.
Draw a circle with a gap (Queue). Put a new number in. Draw arrows showing the existing numbers "stepping over" the new one to get behind it.
Draw a tree. Draw a vertical dashed line through the root. Use curved arrows to show the left child moving to the right and vice versa at every level.
Draw the expression. Draw a stack. When you see '*' or '/', pop the top, multiply/divide with current, and push back. Addition just pushes the number.
Draw the array. Draw a bracket `[` at the start. Move the `]` right until the gap is > 1. Write the range. Jump `[` to the next element.
Draw two boxes (Candidate A, Candidate B). For each new number, show it "attacking" the boxes if it doesn't match, or "reinforcing" if it does.

Problem Title: Kth Smallest Element in a BST

Draw two buckets side-by-side. Label one "Input" and one "Output". Draw an arrow showing a one-way "pour" only when "Output" is dry.
Draw N as a set of slots (e.g., [1][2][3][4]). For each slot, calculate the "1-cycles" based on the power of 10 for that position.

Problem Title: Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Tree
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a tree. Use arrows pointing UP from P and Q. Where two arrows meet in a single node, that's your LCA.

Problem Title: Delete Node in a Linked List

Draw N at the top. Branch out to its factors. For each factor, branch out again. Highlight paths that end in a "1" or prime factor.

Draw a tree. Trace a line down the leftmost path. At each node, write the path "1->2". When moving back up, erase/pop the last node from your mental path list.
Draw a circle with 9 points (1 to 9). Show that adding digits is equivalent to "spinning" around this circle.
Draw a sorted array. Circle index i. Place two arrows (L and R). If sum is small, draw a bracket covering everyone between L and R to show they are all counted instantly.

How to Draw (Opt): Draw a box labeled "Slots". Put the number 1 in it. As you read the string, erase and update the number. Use arrows pointing from the string to the box.
Draw the airports as circles. Draw tickets as arrows. As you traverse an arrow, put a cross on it. When stuck, write the airport name in a list and "pop" back to the previous airport.
Draw a tree. Start at the leaves. Write the 4-tuple `[T, 1, val, val]` next to them. Move up to the parent and show the math comparing the tuples to form a new tuple.
Draw two boxes labeled `Small` and `Mid`. Write ∞ in both. As you scan the array, update the boxes. If you hit a number that fits nowhere, "winner" flag is raised.
Draw three types of spirals: "Expanding", "Shrinking", and "Stalled". Mark the specific points where the "Shrinking" line hits the "Expanding" boundary.

Optimised Pattern: Trie (Prefix/Suffix) or Hashing with Word Splitting
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a Trie of reversed words. For a search word "abc", trace "c"->"b"->"a". At each node, check if the "remaining" part of the original word in the Trie is a palindrome.
Draw a tree. Starting from leaves, write a pair [1, 0] (if value is 1). For the parent, show the addition logic to create its own [rob, skip] pair.

Optimised Pattern: Dynamic Programming (Most Significant Bit / Least Significant Bit)
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a 1D array. For index 7 (111), show an arrow pointing to index 3 (011) and adding the +1 from the last bit.
Draw the nested list. Draw concentric circles around the deeper brackets. Write the "weight" as a multiplier next to each circle.

Problem Title: Longest Substring with At Most K Distinct Characters
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the string. Draw a sliding "box" (window). Below it, draw a small Hash Map table. Show the box expanding right until the map has k+1 entries, then show the left side "shrinking".
Draw a vertical stack. Put the initial list inside. Show the list "expanding" into its components within the stack. Use a "Pointer" arrow at the top of the stack.
Draw a 32-bit binary string. Highlight positions 0, 2, 4, etc. Draw an AND operation with the input bit to show if the "1" falls in a valid slot.

Optimised Pattern: Dynamic Programming or Mathematical Optimization (Rule of 3s)
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the number n as a long bar. Chop it into segments of length 3. If a small bit is left, show it merging into a 4 to be split into 2 x 2.
Draw an array. Place two arrows at the ends. Draw a curved "swap" arrow between the letters they point to. Move arrows inward.
Draw an array. Draw arrows at the ends. If an arrow points to 'b', move it. If both point to 'a' and 'e', draw a swap arc.
Draw a fixed-size circular track with 3 slots. Show a number entering one side and another "falling out" the other side. Update a central "Sum" box.
Draw a Map (Key arrow Count). Then draw a row of "buckets" indexed 1, 2, 3... Drop the keys into the bucket corresponding to their count. Scan buckets from right to left.
Draw a grid. Outside the grid, draw a vertical array for rows and a horizontal one for columns. When a move is made, just increment the corresponding array slot.
Draw two circles (Sets). Show elements being moved from the arrays into the circles, then highlight the overlapping area.

Optimised Pattern: Frequency Map or Sort + Two Pointers
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a Frequency Map. For `nums2`, draw an arrow. If it hits a key in the map, "eat" one count from that key and add the number to the result tape.
Draw the 3 x 3 grid. Write the number of patterns starting at '1' and multiply by 4. Do the same for '2' (multiply by 4) and '5' (multiply by 1).
Draw a number line with existing segments [1, 3] and [5, 6]. Place x=4. Draw arrows from 4 to the neighboring segments showing they all fuse into [1, 6].
Draw a grid with a snake. Beside it, draw a Deque (horizontal tape) and a HashSet (scattered box). Show the tail "leaving" the HashSet at the same time the head "enters" it.

Optimised Pattern: Sort + Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS) via Binary Search
How to Draw (Opt): Draw envelopes as pairs (w, h). Sort them. Then extract just the h values and draw the "Patience Sorting" piles used in the O(N log N) LIS algorithm.
Draw several vertical "stacks" of tweets (most recent on top). Draw a small Max-Heap box. Show one tweet from the top of each stack being "pulled" into the heap.
Draw a scatter plot. Identify the leftmost and rightmost points. Draw a bold dotted line exactly in the middle. Use arrows to "pair up" dots from the left and right.
Draw n empty slots. Write the number of choices above each: 9, 9, 8, 7... Draw multiplication symbols between them. Underneath, draw a Plus sign to show you are adding results from previous n.
Draw a "Ready" box (Max-Heap) and a "Waiting Room" (Queue). Show a character moving from the box to the string, then sitting in the waiting room for k steps before jumping back to the box.
Draw a table with two columns: "Message" and "Available At". For a new entry, show a "Check" against the clock and an "Update" to the timestamp.
Draw a U-shaped parabola. Place points on the curve. Draw two pointers at the furthest left and right points. Show them "moving down" the curve to the center as you pick values.
Draw a grid. Next to it, draw a single variable `rowCount`. Above each column, draw a small box for `colCount[j]`. Show these numbers updating only when a 'W' is passed.
Draw a circle divided into 300 segments. As a "second" pointer moves around, show old data being erased and replaced by the current second's hits.

Problem Title: Max Sum of Rectangle No Larger Than K
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a grid with two vertical boundary lines. Show the rows between them collapsing into a single vertical 1D column. Beside it, draw a BST (TreeSet) storing cumulative sums.
Draw a nested structure. Below it, draw a "Bucket". Drop all integers from level 1 into it, then add the bucket to the total. Drop level 2 integers into the *same* bucket, then add the bucket again.
Draw two bars of lengths x and y. Draw a small block representing the gcd(x, y). Show that any reachable z must be composed of an integer number of these small blocks.
Draw a tree. At each node, write its height (Max(Left, Right) + 1). Draw arrows pointing from nodes to a vertical list of "buckets" indexed by those height numbers.
Draw a bar from 1 to N. Draw a line in the middle. Show an arrow discarding the half that doesn't contain the root. Repeat.

Optimised Pattern: Dynamic Programming (Sort + Longest Path in DAG)
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a sorted array. Draw arrows from each number to its divisors on the left. Write the length of the longest chain above each number.
Draw a list like 1 arrow 2 arrow 9 arrow 9. Place a "Point" arrow on the '2'. Change 2 to 3, and then change all 9s after it to 0s in one pass.
Draw an array. For an update [+3] on range [1, 3], put "+3" at index 1 and "-3" at index 4. Then draw a "Summing Wave" moving through the array to produce the final values.
Draw two binary strings. Underneath, draw the XOR result and the AND-Shift result. Show the shift "carrying" bits to the next column until the carry line is all zeros.
Draw a recursion tree where each node processes the last digit of the array and passes the remaining array up. Show the base^{10} and base^{digit} branches.

Problem Title: Find K Pairs with Smallest Sums
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a grid. Circle the top-left cell. Draw two arrows pointing right and down. Use a "Priority Queue" box to store the tips of these arrows, always picking the smallest.
Draw a bar. Cut it in half. Shade the rejected half. Repeat until only one number remains.

Problem Title: Guess Number Higher or Lower II
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a 2D matrix where the axes are i and j. Show the diagonal (length 1) being 0, then compute length 2, then length 3, filling the upper triangle.
Draw a series of dots on a graph. Draw a zig-zag line connecting them. Circle only the points where the line changes direction (peaks and valleys).
Draw a 1D array from 0 to Target. For index 4, draw arrows pointing back to (4-1), (4-2), etc., and sum their values into the current cell.

Problem Title: Kth Smallest Element in a Sorted Matrix
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a grid. Draw a "staircase" line separating elements le mid from those > mid. Show the binary search halving the value range.
Draw a "Waiting Line" (Queue) of numbers. Draw a "Busy Room" (HashSet). Show a number moving from the line to the room and back.
Draw a List and a Map. To delete "B", show the last element "Z" moving into "B"'s slot. Update the Map to point "Z" to the new index. Erase the tail.

Problem Title: Insert Delete GetRandom O(1) - Duplicates allowed
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a List and a Map. If deleting '2', show the Map pointing to indices 1, 2. Pick index 1. Swap List[1] with the last element. Update the Map to show the last element's new index.
Draw a list. As you move the pointer, draw a "Dice" icon. At node 1, you pick it. At node 2, you flip a coin to decide if you keep 1 or switch to 2. Label the "Current Winner" box.
Draw a 26-slot array (a-z). Fill it with counts from the magazine. For each letter in the note, show the number in the corresponding slot "dropping" by one.
Draw an array. Draw a vertical dashed line (the boundary). To the left, "Shuffled". Pick a random element from the right, swap it to the boundary, and move the line.
Draw a string. Below it, draw a vertical Stack. As you hit '[', show a new "Box" being pushed onto the stack. When you hit ']', show the "Box" being placed inside the box below it.
Draw a root node. Draw branches 1, 2, 3... Under 1, draw branches 0, 1, 2... (forming 10, 11, 12). Under 10, draw 0, 1... (forming 100, 101). Show the "DFS Path" line.

Problem Title: First Unique Character in a String
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a 26-slot array (a-z). As you scan the string, increment the counts. Then draw a pointer moving through the string again, checking the count in the array for each character.
Draw a vertical stack. As you see a directory, push its length. If a new directory has a smaller tab count, pop until the stack matches the depth. When you see a dot (file), calculate the total.
Draw two strings. Draw one large XOR gate. Show all characters from both strings going into the gate. Show the single resulting character coming out.
Draw a number line. Mark the `head` with an arrow. In each step, show the `head` jumping or staying put, while the distance between potential numbers (the step) doubles.
Draw 4 small rectangles forming one large one. Circle the points where they meet. Show that the meeting points have 2 or 4 corners overlapping, while the 4 outside corners only have 1.
Draw two horizontal strings. Draw an arrow i on s and j on t. Draw a vertical "match" line only when the letters are identical, then advance both.
Draw an 8-bit box. Show an AND operation with `10000000` to check the first bit. Use a "Counter" variable to keep track of how many `10......` bytes are needed.
Draw a vertical stack. As you hit `[`, show a "snapshot" of the current string being pushed. As you hit `]`, show the current working string being multiplied and "glued" to the one below it.

Problem Title: Longest Substring with At Least K Repeating Characters
Optimised Pattern: Divide and Conquer (Split on Invalid Characters)
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a long bar (the string). Find a "bad" character like 'z' in the middle. Draw a line through 'z', breaking the bar into two smaller bars. Repeat until all characters in a bar are valid.
Write the full formula for F(0) and F(1). Subtract them on paper to reveal that the entire array sum is added, while the last element is subtracted n times.
Draw n in binary (e.g., 15 = 1111_2). Show that adding 1 makes it 10000_2, which allows 4 consecutive divisions, whereas subtracting 1 only allows one.
Draw the array. Move a pointer. Every time it hits the target, draw a "Dice" and show the probability 1/k of updating the "Result" box.

Optimised Pattern: Graph + BFS/DFS or Disjoint Set Union (DSU) with Weights
How to Draw (Opt): Draw nodes (A, B, C). Draw an edge A to B with weight 2.0. Draw B to C with weight 3.0. To find A/C, draw a path A to B to C and write 2.0 x 3.0 = 6.0.
Draw three horizontal bars labeled "1-digit", "2-digits", "3-digits". Show n "jumping" over the first two bars and landing in the middle of the third.

Optimised Pattern: Backtracking Iterate through the 10 LEDs, choosing to turn them on/off until `turnedOn` is met.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw 10 circles (LEDs). Write '1' or '0' inside them. Keep a running tally of active LEDs.

Optimised Pattern: Monotonic Stack Keep digits in increasing order. Pop if current digit < top of stack and K > 0.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a 'U' shape for the stack. Write numbers vertically. Physically scratch out the top number when popping. Keep a 'K' tally.

Optimised Pattern: DP (Hash Map of Sets) Map each stone to a Set of jump lengths that reached it.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw circles in a line for stones. Above each circle, draw a small box (the Set). Write incoming jump sizes into the box.

Optimised Pattern: DFS/BFS Traversal (Same as Brute force, as you must visit every node to check).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a binary tree. Mark left edges with 'L'. Circle nodes that have an incoming 'L' edge and no outgoing edges.

Optimised Pattern: Bit Manipulation Group every 4 bits using bitwise AND (num & 15), then right shift by 4 (num >>> 4).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw 32 boxes (bits). Bracket them in groups of 4. Draw arrows from each group pointing to a Hex character.

Optimised Pattern: Sort + Insert Sort descending by height (h), then ascending by (k). Insert people into a list at index k.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw boxes representing people (h, k). Draw the tallest array first. Draw arrows showing shorter boxes wedging between existing ones.

Optimised Pattern: Min-Heap + BFS Push perimeter to min-heap. Pop lowest boundary, process neighbors, add to heap. "Shrinking perimeter".
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a 2D grid top-down. Circle perimeter cells. Cross out the lowest number, draw an arrow to an inner neighbor, calculate trapped water, circle neighbor.

Optimised Pattern: Two Pointers Pointer `i` traverses the word, `j` traverses abbreviation. Parse numbers to skip `i` forward.
How to Draw (Opt): Write the word and abbreviation aligned. Draw arrows for `i` and `j` underneath. Draw a long arcing jump for `i` when `j` hits a digit.

Optimised Pattern: Hash Map / Frequency Array Count char frequencies. Add all even counts. Add (odd-1) for odd counts. Add 1 if any odd exists.
How to Draw (Opt): Write down letters. Tally them. Draw circles around pairs of 2. Draw one distinct box for a leftover character to sit in the center.

Optimised Pattern: Binary Search on Answer Binary search the "max sum" (Low: max(arr), High: sum(arr)). Greedily count chunks needed for each guess.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a number line for Binary Search. Pick a Mid. Draw an array. Draw 'U' shaped buckets. Fill buckets with numbers up to Mid. Count buckets.

Optimised Pattern: Bitmask + Backtracking Find character differences as bitmasks. Use DFS to find the shortest abbreviation mask that covers all difference constraints.
How to Draw (Opt): Write the target word. Below it, write dict words. Draw vertical lines connecting differing chars. Write binary bits (1s and 0s) next to them to represent conflicts.

Optimised Pattern: String Concatenation (Better for scale/many conditions). Append "Fizz" if mod 3, append "Buzz" if mod 5. Output if empty.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a horizontal line (belt). Draw downward arrows labeled '3' and '5'. Write the resulting word building up block-by-block left to right.

Optimised Pattern: 1D DP / Two Pointers If arr[i]-arr[i-1] == arr[i-1]-arr[i-2], current slices = prev slices + 1. Add to total sum.
How to Draw (Opt): Write the array. Draw 'V' shapes between numbers with the difference. When 'V' numbers match consecutively, underline them and write "+1, +2, +3".

Optimised Pattern: Top-3 Variables Keep `first`, `second`, `third` initialized to -Infinity. Update them sequentially as you iterate O(N).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw 3 vertically stacked boxes. When a new number arrives, physically cross out the values and draw arrows pushing the old values down one box.

Optimised Pattern: Two Pointers (Right to Left) Traverse both strings backwards. Add digits plus carry. Append to StringBuilder and reverse.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw strings stacked vertically. Draw vertical lines for columns. Circle the column sum. Draw a swooping left arrow to carry the '1' to the next column.

Optimised Pattern: 0/1 Knapsack DP (1D) Use a boolean array `dp` where `dp[i]` is true if sum `i` is possible.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a 1D horizontal grid. For each number in the input, draw arrows from existing 'T' cells to new 'T' cells. Write from right-to-left.

Optimised Pattern: Multi-source BFS/DFS Start from the Pacific edges and "flow uphill." Do the same for Atlantic. Find the intersection.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw two empty grids. In Grid 1, shade everything reachable from top/left. In Grid 2, shade from bottom/right. Overlay them.

Optimised Pattern: DP / Pre-calculated Jumps Join sentence with spaces. At each screen column start, pre-calculate which word the next row will start with.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a circle with words on the perimeter. Draw "jump" arrows from different starting points to their landing points after one 'Cols' length.

Optimised Pattern: Single Pass (Head Counting) Iterate through the grid. Only count an 'X' if it doesn't have an 'X' above it or to its left.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a grid. For an 'X', check the cell above and the cell to the left. If both are empty/off-board, circle it. If not, ignore.

Optimised Pattern: Greedy + Case Analysis Categorize by length: <6 (Insert), 6-20 (Replace), >20 (Delete then Replace). Priority on deleting from repeating triplets.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw three buckets labeled "Insert", "Delete", "Replace". Put counts in them based on 3 specific math rules for each length category.

Optimised Pattern: Bitwise Trie Insert all numbers into a Binary Trie. For each number, traverse the trie trying to pick the opposite bit.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a binary tree where each node has only '0' and '1' branches. Trace a path for number X, trying to go left if X's bit is 1 and right if 0.

Optimised Pattern: Direct Indexing Compare `words[i].charAt(j)` with `words[j].charAt(i)` with boundary checks.
How to Draw (Opt): Write words in a grid. Use a ruler to look at the 3rd row, then rotate the ruler 90 degrees to check the 3rd column.

Optimised Pattern: Unique Char Counting Observe unique identifiers: 'z' is only in zero, 'w' in two, 'x' in six. Subtract counts in a specific order.
How to Draw (Opt): List numbers 0-9. Next to each, write its "Unique Character" (e.g., 0: 'z', 2: 'w', 4: 'u'). Draw arrows "subtracting" counts from a master frequency map.

Optimised Pattern: Sliding Window (Expand/Contract) Use two pointers. Keep track of `maxFreq` in current window. If `windowSize - maxFreq > k`, shrink left.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a string. Draw a bracket [ dots ] over a section. Below, write a tally of chars. If (total - max) > k, shift the left bracket.

Optimised Pattern: Trie-Based Backtracking Use a Trie to instantly find all words that start with a specific prefix (the prefix being the current column's characters).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw an empty 4 x 4 grid. Write "BALL" in row 1. Draw arrows from Row 1's letters to the first letters of subsequent rows.

Optimised Pattern: In-Place In-order Maintain a `first` and `last` pointer. Connect `last.right = current` and `current.left = last` during traversal.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a BST. Use a red pen to trace the in-order path. At each node, draw dotted lines to its predecessor and successor.

Optimised Pattern: Divide & Conquer Recursively check if a quadrant is "leaf". If all 4 children are leaves and same value, merge.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw an 8x8 grid. Draw a cross splitting it to 4x4s. In one 4x4, if all bits are '1', write 'Leaf'. If mixed, split again.

Optimised Pattern: Pre-order with Child Count Serialize as `Value, ChildCount`. E.g., `1,3,2,0,3,0,4,0` (Node 1 has 3 children...).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a tree. Next to each node, write the number of its children. Follow the pre-order path and write the pairs (Val, Count).

Optimised Pattern: BFS (Queue) Use a Queue. Process all nodes in the current queue (one level) before moving to the next.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a tree. Circle the root (Level 0). Draw a horizontal line. Circle all its children (Level 1). Draw another line.

Optimised Pattern: Iterative Stack / Pointer Weaving When you hit a `child`, "insert" the child branch between `current` and `current.next`.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a list with a "down" branch. Draw an arrow from `current.next` to the end of the `child` branch, and from `current` to the `child` head.

Problem Title: Encode N-ary Tree to Binary Tree
Optimised Pattern: LCRS (Recursive Pointer Re-assignment) Use a recursive DFS to transform pointers without creating new nodes.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw an N-ary tree. Connect siblings with red lines. Erase the original child-parent lines except for the first child.

Optimised Pattern: Hash Map + Doubly Linked List of Buckets Map strings to nodes. Nodes are buckets of strings with the same count. Buckets are linked in order of count.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a horizontal DLL where each node contains a Set of strings. Use arrows from a separate Hash Map pointing into these Set nodes.

Optimised Pattern: BFS (Shortest Path) Use a Queue. For each mutation, check if it's in the "bank". Use a visited set to avoid cycles.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw nodes for genes. Connect genes that differ by 1 letter. Trace the shortest hop-count path from Start to End.

Problem Title: Number of Segments in a String
Optimised Pattern: Single Pass Count Iterate through characters. Count a segment whenever you see a non-space character that follows a space (or is at index 0).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a string. Draw a dot above every character that is NOT a space but HAS a space (or nothing) to its left.

Optimised Pattern: Greedy (Sort by End Time) Sort intervals by their end times. Always pick the one that ends earliest to leave maximum room for others.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw intervals on a timeline. Mark "X" through any interval that starts before the current "Last End Time."

Optimised Pattern: Binary Search + Sort Store all `start` values in a sorted list with original indices. For each `end`, binary search the list.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw two horizontal lists. List A (unsorted intervals). List B (sorted starts). Draw an arrow from List A[i].end into List B using "High/Low" markers.

Optimised Pattern: Prefix Sum + DFS Use a Hash Map to store "Prefix Sum -> Frequency". If (CurrentSum - Target) exists in the map, a path is found.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a tree path. Next to each node, write the running sum. Maintain a small "Tally Table" (the Map) on the side. Strike out entries when you backtrack.

Problem Title: Find All Anagrams in a String
Optimised Pattern: Sliding Window + Frequency Map Use an array of size 26. As the window moves, increment the "entering" char and decrement the "leaving" char.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw two 26-slot boxes. Write the target counts. Draw a sliding window over the string, updating the counts and checking for a match.

Optimised Pattern: Stack (Right-to-Left) Scan the string backwards. If you hit a `?`, pop the next two values from the stack and push the result based on the condition.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the string. Scan backwards. Draw a "Stack" bucket. Push numbers/chars. When you hit `?`, draw a circle around the top two stack items and replace them.

Optimised Pattern: Denary Tree Traversal Visualize numbers as a 10-ary tree (1, 10, 100...). Calculate "steps" between prefixes to skip whole subtrees.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a root '1'. Draw 10 children. Label how many nodes are under '1' (e.g., 10, 11, 100...). Use subtraction to "jump" K across siblings.

Optimised Pattern: Binary Search / Math Search for k such that frac{k(k+1)}{2} le N or use the quadratic formula directly.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a number line. Mark L, M, and R. Calculate the sum for M and cross out half the line in each step.

Problem Title: Find All Duplicates in an Array
Optimised Pattern: In-place Marking For each number x, go to index |x|-1 and flip the value to negative. If already negative, it's a duplicate.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw an array. For index 0, look at its value (say 4). Go to index 4. Draw a circle around it and add a minus sign.

Optimised Pattern: Two Pointers (Read/Write) Use `read` and `write` pointers. `read` finds the end of a group; `write` updates the array in-place.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw an array. Draw an arrow R moving fast and an arrow W moving slow. Label W as the "New Length" tracker.

Optimised Pattern: Topological Sort (Kahn's) Build a graph of dependencies. Check if at every step there is exactly 1 node with an in-degree of 0.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw circles (numbers) and arrows (dependencies). Keep an "In-degree" table on the side. Scratch off counts as you process nodes.

Optimised Pattern: Two Stacks Push all nodes of both lists onto two stacks. Pop from both to add from right-to-left.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw two vertical buckets. Fill them with numbers. Draw arrows popping from the top and summing them into a new Linked List.

Optimised Pattern: DP with Map Matrix Use an array of HashMaps `dp[i]`, where `dp[i][diff]` stores count of slices ending at i with difference `diff`.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw an array. Below each element, draw a small vertical list of (diff: count) pairs. Draw arrows from previous indices' lists to current.

Optimised Pattern: Hash Map of Distances For each point i, calculate distances to all other points j. Store counts in a Map `dist: count`. Result is sum count x (count - 1).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw points on a plane. Pick one point. Draw concentric circles passing through other points. Count points per circle and apply n(n-1).

Problem Title: Find All Numbers Disappeared in an Array
Optimised Pattern: In-place Negation For each value x, treat |x|-1 as an index and make the value at that index negative. Positives remaining are the gaps.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw an array. For index 0, read value 4. Go to index 3 (4-1). Add a minus sign. Repeat. Circle indices that stay positive.

Optimised Pattern: Pre-order + Bounds Use Pre-order without null markers. Rebuild using BST property (current value must be within [min, max]).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a BST. Write its pre-order sequence. Reconstruct by drawing nodes only if they fit between the parent's limits.

Optimised Pattern: Recursive Re-linking Find node. If it's a leaf, remove. If 1 child, replace with child. If 2 children, replace with In-order Successor (min in right subtree).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a BST. Circle the node to delete. Find the smallest node in its right branch. Draw an arrow moving it into the deleted spot.

Optimised Pattern: Bucket Sort Use an array where the index is the frequency. Put characters into the bucket at `frequency[char]` index.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw an array where indices are 0, 1, dots, N. Write chars inside the boxes. Scan the array backwards to build the string.

Problem Title: Minimum Number of Arrows to Burst Balloons
Optimised Pattern: Greedy (Sort by End) Sort balloons by their end coordinates. Shoot one arrow at the end of the first balloon; skip all that overlap.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw bars on a timeline. Sort by end. Draw a vertical line at the end of the first bar. Circle all bars hit. Repeat for the next uncircled bar.

Problem Title: Minimum Moves to Equal Array Elements
Optimised Pattern: Mathematical Reduction Incrementing n-1 elements is mathematically equivalent to decrementing 1 element. Sum up the distance of each element from the minimum.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw towers of different heights. Draw a dotted line at the height of the shortest tower. Sum the lengths of the "cut off" sections.

Optimised Pattern: Hash Map (Meet-in-the-Middle) Calculate all A+B and store in a Map `sum: count`. Then iterate C+D and check for -(C+D) in the Map.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw two groups of arrays. Group 1: Draw a Map of sums. Group 2: Draw arrows checking their negative sums against the Map.

Optimised Pattern: Two Pointers + Greedy Sort both arrays. Give the smallest cookie that satisfies the child with the smallest greed factor.
How to Draw (Opt): Write both arrays. Sort them. Draw an arrow c (child) and k (cookie). Move them and tally matches.

Optimised Pattern: Monotonic Stack (Right-to-Left) Keep a stack of potential "2" values (middle magnitude) and track the largest "3" value found so far.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a line graph of the numbers. Use a vertical "Stack" bucket. As you scan left, pop smaller values to find the "next greater" value.

Optimised Pattern: Two Pointers (Slow/Fast) Use Floyd's Cycle-Finding algorithm. A fast pointer moves 2 steps, slow moves 1. Check for direction changes or single-node loops.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a "Clock Face" with array indices. Draw jump arrows. Move a "S" and "F" marker. If they land on the same spot, a loop exists.

Optimised Pattern: Information Theory (N-Dimensional Hypercube) Each pig represents a dimension. The number of states a pig can have is (Tests + 1). Solve (States)^{Pigs} ge Buckets.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a 2D grid for 2 pigs. Label rows for Pig 1 and columns for Pig 2. Each cell is a bucket. Pig 1 drinks row 1 in round 1, etc.

Optimised Pattern: String Doubling Trick Concatenate S+S. Remove the first and last characters. If S is still a substring of the result, it has a repeated pattern.
How to Draw (Opt): Write the string S twice. Shift the second S by one character. If the internal sequence matches the original S, return true.

Optimised Pattern: Hash Map + Linked List of DLLs Map keys to nodes. Have a second "Frequency Map" that points to a Doubly Linked List of all keys with that frequency.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a vertical DLL of frequencies. From each frequency node, draw a horizontal DLL of keys. Draw a separate Map pointing directly to key nodes.

Optimised Pattern: Bitwise XOR + Bit Counting XOR the two numbers (x oplus y). Count the set bits using Brian Kernighan’s algorithm.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw two binary rows. Draw a third row (XOR) where 1 marks a difference. Circle the 1s and tally.

Problem Title: Min Moves to Equal Elements II
Optimised Pattern: Median Target (Greedy) The point that minimizes the sum of absolute differences is the median. Sort and find the middle.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a number line with dots. Draw arrows from outermost dots toward the center. Where they meet (median) is the goal.

Optimised Pattern: Single Pass (Shared Edge) Count land cells x 4. Subtract 2 for every shared edge (horizontal or vertical neighbor).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw two adjacent boxes. Write "4" in each. Draw a thick line where they touch and write "-2". Total = 6.

Optimised Pattern: Recursion + Bitmask Memoization Use an integer bitmask to represent which numbers are used. Cache result in a Map `mask: win/loss`.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a binary bitmask (e.g., `1011`). Next to it, write "T/F". Show arrows from different branches leading to the same bitmask.

Optimised Pattern: Backtracking on Net Balances Flatten everyone into their net balance. Try to settle one person's balance with any other person of the opposite sign.
How to Draw (Opt): List net balances: `[10, -5, -2, -3]`. Start with 10. Give 5 to the second person. Update list and move to the next index.

Optimised Pattern: Cycle Detection (Pigeonhole) Record the `index` in s_2 after each s_1. If the same index repeats, a cycle is found. Calculate remainder math.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a timeline. Mark points where s_1 ends. Note the pointer position in s_2. Circle the repeating sequence of positions.

Optimised Pattern: DP (Max Length per Char) Track the longest "consecutive" sequence ending at each character ('a' through 'z'). The sum of these 26 values is the result.
How to Draw (Opt): Write the string p. Below it, write the "current run" length. Maintain an array of 26 max-lengths. Update the array only if current > existing.

Optimised Pattern: Regex or Modular Parsing Use pre-defined Regex patterns for IPv4 and IPv6 to handle boundaries and valid character ranges in one pass.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the structure: `X.X.X.X` vs `X:X:X:X:X:X:X:X`. Below each segment, write its specific constraints (e.g., 0-255).

Optimised Pattern: Cross Product (Orientation) For every three consecutive points, calculate the cross product. All cross products must have the same sign (all positive or all negative).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a polygon. Draw arrows between vertices. At each vertex, draw a small arc showing the "turn" direction. All arcs should curve the same way.

Optimised Pattern: Rejection Sampling (7x7 Matrix) Create a range of 1-49 using `7 * (rand7() - 1) + rand7()`. If the result is > 40, reject it. Otherwise, return `result % 10 + 1`.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a 7 x 7 grid on paper. Label cells 1-49. Circle cells 41-49 and write "Reroll."

Optimised Pattern: Interval DP `dp[i][j]` stores the shortest encoded string for `s[i...j]`. Check for patterns using `(s+s).indexOf(s, 1)`.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw an N x N grid. Fill it diagonally starting from length 1. For `dp[0][4]`, show it checking `dp[0][k] + dp[k+1][4]`.

Optimised Pattern: Trie + DFS/Memoization Sort words by length. Insert into a Trie. For each word, use DFS to see if it can be formed by existing Trie words.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a Trie. Trace a path for "catsdogcats". When you hit 'cats', circle the node and draw a second arrow starting back at the root for 'dog'.

Optimised Pattern: DFS with Pruning / Bitmask DP Sort matchsticks descending. Try placing in a side; if it exceeds `target`, backtrack immediately.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw 4 vertical bars. Write "Target" at the top. Draw matchsticks as blocks filling these bars. Cross out configurations that overflow.

Optimised Pattern: 2D Knapsack DP `dp[i][j]` is max strings using `i` zeros and `j` ones. Update backwards to save space.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a 2D grid (e.g., 5 x 3). For a string "001", show moving from `dp[2][1]` to `dp[0][0]` and adding +1 to the count.

Optimised Pattern: Binary Search (Sorted Heaters) Sort heaters. For each house, binary search for the two closest heaters (one left, one right).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a number line. Mark Heaters. For a House X, find the heater index i such that `Heaters[i]` is just above X. Compare with `Heaters[i-1]`.

Optimised Pattern: Bitwise Mask XOR Find the highest set bit. Create a mask of all 1s of that length. Result is `num ^ mask`.
How to Draw (Opt): Write the number in binary. Write the mask (all 1s) directly below it. Draw vertical lines; if both are 1, write 0. If 1 and 0, write 1.

Optimised Pattern: Bit-by-Bit Counting For each bit position (0-31), count how many numbers have a '1' (k) and how many have a '0' (n-k). Distance contribution is k x (n-k).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw 3-4 numbers as 8-bit binary rows. For the first column, count total 1s and 0s. Multiply them. Repeat for all 8 columns.

Problem Title: Generate Random Point in a Circle
Optimised Pattern: Inverse Transform (Polar) Generate theta in [0, 2π] and r = R √{rand()}. The square root is crucial for uniform density.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a circle. Draw two arrows: one for angle theta, one for radius r. Write the formula x = r cos theta, y = r sin theta.

Optimised Pattern: Greedy Palindrome Generation Start from the largest possible palindrome (e.g., 999999) and work downwards. For each, check if it has two n-digit factors.
How to Draw (Opt): Write 999, 998, 997 dots in a column. Next to each, draw its "mirror." For each mirror, show a short trial-division loop.

Optimised Pattern: Two Heaps (Balance) / TreeSet Use a Max-Heap for the left half and a Min-Heap for the right half. Use "Lazy Removal" with a Hash Map to handle elements leaving the window.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw two vertical buckets. Write "Small" and "Large". As you slide, draw a tally of "Pending Deletes" next to the buckets. Rebalance so |Small| approx |Large|.

Optimised Pattern: Two Pointers (Generator/Consumer) Use an `int[]` or `char[]` and two pointers: `head` (reading how many) and `tail` (writing what's next).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a long horizontal array. Mark 'head' at index 2 and 'tail' at the end. Move 'head' forward, look at its value, and write that many characters at 'tail'.

Optimised Pattern: Reverse Traversal Start from the end of the string. Collect K characters, add a dash, and repeat. Reverse the result at the end.
How to Draw (Opt): Write the alphanumeric string. Draw an arrow starting at the last char, moving left. Every K steps, draw a vertical line (dash).

Optimised Pattern: Binary Search on Length The number of '1's can only be between 2 and 64. For each fixed length m, binary search for base k.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a column 2 to 64. For each row, draw a horizontal number line (Binary Search) to find k.

Optimised Pattern: Stack-Based Reversal Use a stack to push 1, 2, 3 dots If you see 'I', pop everything to the result. If you see 'D', keep pushing.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a line 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Above it, draw the pattern `DDI`. Use a bucket (Stack). Push 1, 2, 3. On 'I', empty the bucket: 3, 2, 1.

Optimised Pattern: Single Pass Counter Iterate through the array. Increment `count` if 1; if 0, update `max` and reset `count`.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw an array. Draw an arrow moving left-to-right. Write the current streak count below each element. Circle the largest number.

Optimised Pattern: DP (1D/2D) `dp[i][j]` stores max score diff from subarray i...j. `dp[i][j] = max(nums[i]-dp[i+1][j], nums[j]-dp[i][j-1])`.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw an n x n grid. Fill `dp[i][i] = nums[i]`. Calculate `dp[i][i+1]` using neighboring cells until top-right corner is reached.

Optimised Pattern: Sliding Window (Expandable) Maintain a window with at most one '0'. If zeros > 1, shrink left pointer.
