Problem Title: Maximum Number of Integers to Choose From a Range I
How to Draw (Opt): Write 1 to n. Cross out the banned numbers. Start circling the remaining numbers from left to right. Stop when the sum of circled numbers exceeds maxSum .

Draw the prizes. Slide a window of length k. Write down the "best score so far" in a row of boxes below. Then, at each step, add the "current window score" to the "best score found before this window started."

Problem Title: Disconnect Path in a Binary Matrix by at Most One Flip
Optimised Pattern: Double DFS / Max Flow Min Cut (Path Neutralization).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a grid. Trace a path with a pencil. Erase that pencil line so those cells are now "walls." Try to draw a new path with a pen. If you can't reach the end, one flip is enough.

Problem Title: Maximum Number of Integers to Choose From a Range II (Premium)
Optimised Pattern: Sorting + Binary Search on Answer Space.
How to Draw (Opt): Write the banned numbers. Guess a number X=100. Calculate sum 1...100 using n(n+1)/2. Subtract the sum of banned numbers < 100. If too small, guess X=200. Use binary search to narrow it down.

Problem Title: Take Gifts From the Richest Pile
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a triangle (heap). Put the biggest number at the top. Remove it, root it, put the smaller result back. Watch it "sink" into the triangle. Repeat k times.
Draw the strings. Below them, write '1' or '0'. Below that, write the running total (e.g., 0, 1, 1, 2, 3...). To find the count between two points, just subtract the two numbers at those points.

Optimised Pattern: Binary Search on Answer Space + Greedy Counting.
How to Draw (Opt): Write the house values. Pick a target X=10. Go through the list: "Can I rob this house? (Is it <= 10 and neighbor not robbed?)". Count how many. If count >= k, try X=5.

Optimised Pattern: Frequency Mapping + Greedy Pairing + Min-Cost Observation.
How to Draw (Opt): List the fruits that need to be moved from Basket A and those from Basket B. Sort them. Compare the cost of swapping them directly vs. using the global cheapest fruit twice. Sum the minimums.
Draw the array. Put left and right fingers on the ends. Calculate the "merged" value. Move fingers inward. If they meet on one number, just add that number.

Problem Title: Count the Number of Fair Pairs
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a sorted array. Pick a number. Use two "boundary markers" to find the first and last numbers that, when added to your picked number, stay within the limits. Count the distance between markers.

Optimised Pattern: Precomputation of All Substrings (up to 30 bits) + Hash Map.
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the string. Write down every number formed by 1-3 digits. Index them: "1" at 0, "10" at 0, "101" at 0, "0" at 1... . To answer a query, just check your index list.
Draw strings s and t . Match t from the left as far as possible. Match t from the right as far as possible. Find where the "left-match" and "right-match" can meet or overlap.

Problem Title: Maximum Difference by Remapping a Digit
How to Draw (Opt): Write the number. For Max: Circle the first digit that isn't a 9. Turn every digit like it into a 9. For Min: Circle the first digit. Turn every digit like it into a 0. Subtract the results.

Problem Title: Minimum Score by Changing Two Elements
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a sorted line of numbers. Pick the 3 smallest and 3 largest. Draw brackets for the three scenarios above and measure the width of the remaining "active" numbers.
Write the array. Check: Is 1 there? Yes. Is 2 there? Yes. Is 4 there? No. Answer is 4.
Draw a tree where each leaf is a bit. Each parent shows the sum of 1s below it. When flipping a range, just flip the parent and mark a "Pending Flip" tag on the children.

Problem Title: Merge Two 2D Arrays by Summing Values
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the two sorted lists. Place a finger at the start of each. Move the finger pointing to the smaller ID. If they match, sum the values. Record the results on a third piece of paper.

Problem Title: Minimum Operations to Reduce an Integer to 0
How to Draw (Opt): Write the number in binary. Circle clusters of 1s. If a cluster has length 1, cross it out. If length > 1, add 1 to the end of it and handle the carry. Count each "fix" as one operation.

Problem Title: Count the Number of Square-Free Subsets
How to Draw (Opt): Draw 10 slots (for the 10 primes). Take a number like 6 (mask: 2 and 3). If you have a subset already using 5, you can add 6. If you have one already using 2, you can't. Record the number of ways in a table of 2^10 (1024) rows.

Draw N circles. Look at the LCP matrix: if `LCP[i][j] > 0`, draw a line between circle i and circle j. Color connected circles with the same letter. Then, double-check that the "prefix length" rules are actually followed.
Write the array. Calculate the total. Start a "Running Left Sum" at 0. At each number, calculate `Total - LeftSum - Current`. Write the result. Add `Current` to `LeftSum`.

Problem Title: Find the Divisibility Array of a String
How to Draw (Opt): Write "121", m=3. Start with 0. 1st digit: (0 x 10 + 1) % 3 = 1. 2nd digit: (1 x 10 + 2) % 3 = 0. 3rd digit: (0 x 10 + 1) % 3 = 1. Result: `[0, 1, 0]`.

Problem Title: Find the Maximum Number of Marked Indices
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a sorted array. Draw a vertical line in the middle. Put your left finger at the very start and right finger at the middle line. If the right is >= 2x the left, "match" them and move both fingers. If not, only move the right finger.

Problem Title: Minimum Time to Visit a Cell In a Grid
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the grid. In each cell, write the earliest time you can reach it. Use a priority queue to always expand the cell with the smallest time. If you hit a wall too early, "ping-pong" between your current and previous cell to waste time.

Write the number 4325. Sort the digits: 2, 3, 4, 5. Write two empty slots: `_ _` and `_ _`. Put the 2 in the first slot of Num A, 3 in the first slot of Num B, 4 in the second of Num A, 5 in the second of Num B. Add 24 + 35.

Problem Title: Count Total Number of Colored Cells
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the diamond for n=3. Notice it looks like a 3 x 3 grid but with the corners missing? Or look at it as a 3 x 3 square plus a 2 x 2 square nested inside. Write the formula: 3^2 + 2^2 = 13.

Problem Title: Count Ways to Group Overlapping Ranges
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the intervals as horizontal lines. If two lines overlap, "glue" them together. Count how many separate "glued" segments you have. Calculate 2 to the power of that count.

Optimised Pattern: Tree DP with Rerooting (Change of Root).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a small tree. Root it at 0. Write down the score. Move the root to an adjacent node. Only look at the edge connecting the old root and new root. Flip its direction and update the score.
Write down N and `time`. Subtract (N-1) repeatedly until you can't. If you subtracted an even number of times, start at 1 and add the remainder. If odd, start at N and subtract the remainder.

Problem Title: Kth Largest Sum in a Binary Tree
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a tree. Draw horizontal lines through each level. Add the numbers on each line. Write the sums in a list. Sort the list and pick the k-th largest.

Problem Title: Split the Array to Make Coprime Products
Optimised Pattern: Prime Factorization + Range Merging / Interval Coverage.
How to Draw (Opt): Write the array. Below each number, list its primes. For each prime (like 2), draw a line from the first time it appears to the last. Look for a vertical gap where no lines are crossing.

Problem Title: Number of Ways to Earn Points
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a row of boxes 0 to `target`. Start with box 0 = 1. For each item type, update the boxes by adding values from "backwards" boxes that are a multiple of `val` away.

Problem Title: Count the Number of Vowel Strings in Range
Optimised Pattern: Direct Linear Scan with Set Lookup.
How to Draw (Opt): Write down the indices from left to right . Under each, write the string. Mark the first and last character. Tick the ones that match.

Problem Title: Rearrange Array to Maximize Prefix Score
How to Draw (Opt): Write the numbers from largest to smallest. Below them, write the cumulative sum. Count how many numbers stay above zero.

Problem Title: Count the Number of Beautiful Subarrays
How to Draw (Opt): Write the array. Calculate a running XOR (e.g., 0, 3, 1, 3...). Use a tally sheet (Map) to count how many times you've seen each result. If you see '3' for the second time, it means one beautiful subarray was just completed.

Problem Title: Minimum Time to Complete All Tasks
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a timeline. Sort tasks by end-point. For the first task, mark the last few available seconds. For the second task, count how many marks are already in its range, then add more marks starting from its end-point.
Draw a Map. Key 1 points to a list of tasks. Sort that list by the "Date" field. When a task is completed, change its status but keep it in the list (or remove it).
Draw 5 circles. Put 1 in each. You have X left. Put 7 in the first circle, then the second... Stop if you have 3 left for the last person or if you run out of money.
Draw two identical sorted rows of numbers. Put left finger on Row 1, right finger on Row 2. If right is bigger than left, move both fingers and tally. If not, only move the right finger.

Problem Title: Find Score of an Array After Marking All Elements
Optimised Pattern: Sorting with Index Tracking + Boolean Marking.
How to Draw (Opt): Write the array. List all numbers on the side from smallest to largest. Cross out the smallest number on your list AND its neighbors in the array. Move to the next smallest *not* crossed out.
Pick a time (e.g., 100 minutes). If rank is 4, 4*n^2 <= 100 rightarrow n^2 <= 25 rightarrow n=5 cars. Calculate for all mechanics. If they can do >= target cars, 100 is "Safe." Try 50.

Problem Title: Number of Even and Odd Bits
How to Draw (Opt): Write the number. Look at the last digit. Is it 1? If yes, tally it (count if you've moved an even or odd number of times). Cross out the last digit. Repeat.
Write a list of numbers 0, 1, 2... Next to each, write its (row, col). Check the "L-step" (2 squares one way, 1 square the other) between each pair in the list.

Optimised Pattern: Recursive Backtracking with Frequency Map (or House Robber DP).
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a decision tree. At each number, draw two branches: "Pick" and "Don't Pick." If you "Pick," check your "Already Picked" list to see if the forbidden partner is there. If yes, that branch dies.

Problem Title: Smallest Missing Non-negative Integer After Operations
How to Draw (Opt): Draw "buckets" labeled 0 to value-1 . Put each array number into a bucket based on its remainder. Start at 0. Is there a number in Bucket 0? Yes, take it and move to 1. Is there one in Bucket 1? Yes, take it. Repeat until a bucket is empty.

Write the numbers and a "Running Total." When the total goes negative, look back at the negative numbers you've passed. Pick the biggest "debt" (like -10), cross it out, and add that 10 back to your total.

Problem Title: K Items With the Maximum Sum
How to Draw (Opt): You want the biggest sum. You have a pile of 1 bills, a pile of 0 bills, and a pile of -1 bills. Take k bills. Take all the 1s you can first. If you still need more, take the 0s. If you still need more, you're forced to take the -1s.
Draw `nums` array and `primes` array. Draw arrows from `nums[i]` to valid prime strictly less than `nums[i] - nums[i-1]`.

Problem Title: Minimum Operations to Make All Array Elements Equal
How to Draw (Opt): Draw sorted array and prefix sum array. Draw a vertical line for the query value; compute differences using block sums.

Optimised Pattern: Topological Sorting (Peeling leaves like an onion)
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the graph. Cross out 0-coin leaves. Then draw circle around remaining leaves, cross them out. Repeat once more.

Problem Title: Minimum Time to Eat All Grains
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a 1D number line. Plot hens (H) and grains (G). Draw brackets [ ] around the range each hen covers in time T.

Problem Title: Form Smallest Number From Two Digit Arrays
How to Draw (Opt): Draw two circles (Sets). Find min in overlap. If empty, pick smallest from circle A and circle B and concatenate.

Problem Title: Find the Substring With Maximum Cost
How to Draw (Opt): Write the array of values. Below it, write a running total. Circle the total whenever you write a new maximum. Strike out negatives when you reset.
Draw the array indices in a circle. Draw lines jumping by K. Pull out connected components into separate lists, find median of each.

Optimised Pattern: BFS from every node (or Edge Removal)
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the start node at the center. Draw concentric circles for distances 1, 2, 3. Stop and sum distances when two nodes on the active edge connect.

Problem Title: Find the Longest Balanced Substring of a Binary String
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the string. Below the 0s, draw ascending steps (1, 2, 3). Below the 1s, draw ascending steps (1, 2). Box the matching pair and compute length.

Problem Title: Convert an Array Into a 2D Array With Conditions
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a tally chart for numbers. Draw empty arrays (rows). Deal out numbers like cards: 1st '3' goes to row 0, 2nd '3' goes to row 1, etc.
Write Row 1, Row 2, and Row 3 (Diff). Sort columns by the Diff row. Draw a box around the first K items for Mouse 1.
Draw a number line. Color evens blue, odds red. Draw a bracket [L, R] for the reachable range. Erase numbers in that range from the sets as they are visited.
Plot points on an X/Y axis. Draw a solid vertical line down the middle. Draw dashed lines at `mid - d` and `mid + d`. Only compare points inside the dashed strip.
Draw a square grid. Draw a big 'X' through it highlighting the diagonals. Next to each highlighted cell, write checks up to √Val.
Draw grouped indices on a line (e.g., [0, 2, 5]). Draw a fulcrum moving left to right. Write equations showing Left side += (gap * count), Right side -= (gap * count).

Problem Title: Minimize the Maximum Difference of Pairs
Optimised Pattern: Sort + Binary Search on Answer + Greedy
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the sorted array. Write a "mid" value. Loop through array drawing brackets [ ] around adjacent elements that fit the difference limit. Count brackets.

Problem Title: Minimum Number of Visited Cells in a Grid
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a grid. Label starting cell as '1'. Draw arrows to furthest jumps. Only update a cell's number if the incoming jump count is strictly smaller.

Problem Title: Check if Object Instance of Class (JS)
How to Draw (Opt): Draw an Object box. Draw an arrow pointing up to its Prototype object. Draw an arrow from the Class to its `.prototype`. Check if the arrows ever land on the same box.
Draw an array block. Read length variable (e.g., L=5). Point arrow directly to index 4.
Draw a big box (Outer Function). Put variable `n` inside. Draw a smaller box inside (Inner Function). Draw an arrow from Inner reaching out to `n`.
Draw three boxes: Call Stack, Web APIs, Callback Queue. Draw the timer moving to Web APIs, waiting, then dropping into the Queue to resolve the Promise.
Draw a Hash Map box. Next to each key-value pair, draw a little ticking stopwatch. If a key is overwritten, cross out the old stopwatch and draw a new one.
Draw a toll booth (the Map). Car (args) arrives. Toll booth checks list. If on list, gate opens instantly. If not, car goes to factory, gets a product, toll booth writes it down, gate opens.
Draw an empty grid. Draw arrows: Column 0 points DOWN. Column 1 points UP. Column 2 points DOWN. Fill array numbers strictly along the arrows.
Draw a nested array as a Tree. Top level is depth 0. Draw a horizontal line at depth `n`. Anything above the line gets its brackets removed. Anything below stays intact.
Draw a box labeled "Accumulator". Draw array items on a conveyor belt. Each item drops into the box, changes the number, and the next item drops in.
Draw a timeline. Draw an event as a dot. Start drawing a horizontal line (the timer). If another dot appears, erase the line and start over. When the line reaches length 'T', draw an execution star.
Draw two Trees (Objects). Put a finger on the root of both. Move down to child nodes simultaneously. If fingers point to different types/values, return false.
Draw a pipe. Put array of functions in boxes backwards. Push value `x` in from the right. Draw the transformed value moving left through each box.
Draw a Tree. Root is an empty Map. Edge to child is `arg1`. Edge from child to grandchild is `arg2`. The leaf node contains the cached result. Follow the path.
Draw a set of items. Draw a "Black Box" (the function). Items pass through and fall into labeled buckets (the Map keys).
Draw a tank (the closure). Pour in drops (args). Once the water level hits the "Arity Line" (fn.length), the tank empties (executes).
Draw a Tree. At each node, write its JSON representation. Combine strings as you bubble back up the tree.
Draw the input array. Below it, draw a "Pass/Fail" row based on the function. Draw arrows only from "Pass" items to a new array.
Draw an input array. Above it, draw the "Transformation Function". Draw arrows up, through the function, and into a new array.
Draw a pipe with width `n`. Draw functions entering the pipe. As one exits (resolves), another enters the other side.
Draw two parallel runners: "Worker" and "Clock". Draw a finish line at time `t`. If "Clock" crosses first, the race ends with an Error.

Problem Title: Count the Number of K-Free Subsets
How to Draw (Opt): Draw chains of numbers: e.g., (1 -> 3 -> 5) if K=2. For each chain, calculate subset counts using `dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]`. Multiply results.

Problem Title: Find the Width of Columns of a Grid
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a grid. Draw a vertical rectangle highlighting Column 1. Find the longest string. Move the rectangle to Column 2.

Problem Title: Find the Score of All Prefixes of an Array
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the input array. Below it, write the `runningMax` for each index. Below that, write the sum. Draw an arrow moving left to right.
Draw a tree. Next to each level, write the "Total Sum". At each node, look at its children; subtract their combined sum from the NEXT level's total sum.
Draw the start node. Draw a circle (frontier). Expand the circle to the nearest neighbors first. Label edges with weights.
Draw a matrix of 0s and 1s. To the right of each row, write the count of 1s. Circle the largest number and its row index.
Draw a frequency table for `nums`. For each divisor, look up its multiples in the table and sum their counts.

Problem Title: Minimum Additions to Make Valid String
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the string. Draw a vertical line whenever the alphabetical order breaks (e.g., ab | a | c | b). Count the blocks. Additions = 3 * Blocks - Length.

Problem Title: Minimize the Total Price of the Trips
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the tree. Write a number inside each node representing how many trips pass through it. Use DP: `dp[u][0]` (not halved) and `dp[u][1]` (halved).
Draw a triangle of rows. Label rows 1 to N. Fill the cells according to the observed pattern (e.g., specific cells in the last row, then every few cells in higher rows).
Draw a machine with a lever. Every time the lever is pulled (`next()`), one number pops out. The machine then "freezes" its internal state until the next pull.
Draw a tree. The generator walks down a branch. When it hits a leaf (number), it yields it. It "remembers" its path and resumes from the exact spot.
Draw a conveyer belt (Generator) yielding boxes (Promises). An overseer resolves the box and feeds the result back. If the "Cancel" lever is pulled, the overseer throws a wrench into the belt.
Draw a circle with 24 notches (0-23). Draw an arrow starting at `arrival`, moving `delayed` steps forward. Where it lands is the answer.
Draw three circles (3, 5, 7). Label the overlaps. Use the formula Sum = S(3)+S(5)+S(7) - S(15)-S(21)-S(35) + S(105).
Draw a small array of 101 buckets. As the window slides, increment/decrement buckets. To find beauty, count buckets from the left (index 0) until reaching X.

Problem Title: Minimum Number of Operations to Make All Array Elements Equal to 1
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the array. Pick two numbers, find GCD. Replace one. Repeat until a 1 appears. Use that 1 to "infect" every other number in the array.
Draw a line 0 to N. Draw intervals as bars above the line. "Squash" overlapping bars together. Draw circles in the empty spaces on the line.

Problem Title: Maximum Sum With Exactly K Elements
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the array. Circle the max value `m`. Draw K boxes, starting with `m` and increasing by 1. Sum them using the formula.

Problem Title: Find the Prefix Common Array of Two Arrays
How to Draw (Opt): Draw arrays A and B. Below them, draw a frequency array. As you move right, increment counts for `A[i]` and `B[i]`. If a count hits 2, increment your "common" tracker.

Problem Title: Maximum Number of Fish in a Grid
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a grid with "water" (numbers) and "land" (0). Pick a water cell, "color" the whole connected pond, and sum the values. Move to the next uncolored water cell.
Draw the array with indices. Sort them. Draw arrows jumping between the original indices. Use a BIT to "subtract" the spots where elements were already removed.

Problem Title: Determine the Winner of a Bowling Game
How to Draw (Opt): Draw two rows of scores. For each score, check the previous two. If a 10 exists, write "x2" below the current score. Sum them up and compare.

Problem Title: First Completely Painted Row or Column
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a Coordinate Map (Value -> R, C). Draw two tally bars (Rows, Cols). As you process 'arr', add a tick to the corresponding Row and Col bar. Return when a bar is full.

Problem Title: Minimum Cost of a Path With Special Roads
How to Draw (Opt): Draw only the relevant points (Start, End, and special road endpoints). Draw edges between every pair of points with Manhattan distance. Replace some with special road costs. Find the shortest path.

Draw the string. Point to the last letter. Bump it up by 1. Check the 2 letters to its left. If it matches either, bump it again. Once safe, move to the right and fill with 'a', 'b', or 'c' while avoiding local pairs.
Draw the grid. Number the start cell '0'. Try a move, number it '1'. If stuck, erase '1' and try a different '1'. Continue until the grid is full.
Draw a "Container" (the closure). Put `init` and `val` inside. Draw three buttons on the container: (+), (-), and (Reset). All buttons change the same `val` inside the container.
Draw a gate with a latch. The first person (call) trips the latch and passes through. The latch stays locked for everyone else.
Draw a machine (Outer) that builds a smaller machine (Inner). The Inner machine's only job is to say "Hello World".
Draw the table. Draw brackets around rows with the same Employee ID. Within each bracket, sort by date. Pick the top row from each bracket.
Draw a pile of records. Sort them into stacks by artist name. Count the height of each stack. Arrange stacks from tallest to shortest.
Draw the array. Above it, write the count of unique items seen from the left. Below it, write unique items seen from the right. Subtract the two rows.
Draw two tables. Table A: Number | Freq. Table B: Freq | Count. When adding a number, increment its Freq in A, and update the corresponding tally in B.

Problem Title: Number of Adjacent Elements With the Same Color
How to Draw (Opt): Draw three cells: [i-1, i, i+1]. Only look at these three. If i matches a neighbor, add 1 to a running counter. If it stops matching after a change, subtract 1.

Problem Title: Make Costs of Paths Equal in a Binary Tree
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a tree with leaf values. Pick two sibling leaves. Add to the smaller one to match the larger. Treat their parent as having the new balanced cost. Repeat upward.

Optimised Pattern: Fast and Slow Pointers (Tortoise and Hare)
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a circle. Draw a 'Slow' turtle and a 'Fast' rabbit. When rabbit finishes the loop, the turtle is halfway. Draw new lines to close two smaller loops.

Optimised Pattern: Recursive DFS Flattening + Sorted Key Set
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a nested object. Flatten it: `a: {b: 1}` becomes `a.b: 1`. Collect all unique "a.b" style names, sort them as column headers, and fill the rows.
Draw a timeline. Event 1 starts a "Busy" block. Any events during the block are ignored except the very last one. When the "Busy" block ends, the last one executes and starts a new "Busy" block.
Draw a long loaf of bread. Draw vertical lines at every 'size' interval. Each piece becomes a sub-array in the result.
Draw a 15-character string. Highlight characters at index 11 and 12. "Age is here." Compare it to 60.
Draw a matrix. Sort the numbers in each row. Now look at the vertical columns. Pick the biggest number in each column and add them up.
Draw three rows. Row 1: OR sum from left. Row 2: OR sum from right. Row 3: Current number shifted by K. Combine all three for each index and find the peak.
Draw the sorted array. For each number, calculate its "Power" by multiplying its square with its own value plus a running "weighted sum" of everything to its left.

Problem Title: Find the Losers of the Circular Game
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a clock with N numbers. Start at 0. Jump 1k, then 2k, then 3k. Mark the numbers you land on. Stop when you hit a marked one. List the unmarked ones.
Draw the `derived` array. XOR all numbers together. If the final result is 0, write "True".

Problem Title: Maximum Number of Moves in a Grid
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a grid. Mark column 0 as "active". For column 1, mark cells that can be reached from active cells in column 0 with a strictly larger value. Move to column 2.

Problem Title: Count the Number of Complete Components
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a graph. Highlight a cluster. Count nodes (V) and lines (E). If E = fracV(V-1)2 (undirected), it's a complete component.
Draw a table. Group rows by date. For each group, highlight rows where the two dates match. Divide highlighted count by total count in that group.
Draw buckets for each Bike ID. Toss all ride logs into their buckets. Pull out the log with the highest timestamp from each bucket.
Draw a table. Group rows into buckets by exact User + Date combination. Count the unique courses inside each bucket. Keep buckets with 2+.

Problem Title: Extract Kth Character From The Rope Tree
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a tree where nodes have numbers (lengths). Start at the root. If K is smaller than the left number, follow the left branch. If larger, subtract the left number and go right.
Draw an object with a "Shield" (the Proxy). When a hand (code) reaches for a property that doesn't exist, the Shield "catches" the request and generates a response on the fly.
Draw a tree. Change one leaf. Draw a new root and a new path to that leaf, but draw arrows from the new path pointing to the existing, unchanged branches of the old tree.
Draw an object tree. Pour liquid nitrogen over the root, watching it freeze solid down through all its branches and leaves, rejecting any attempt to bend them.
Draw an Object (Context). Draw a "Temporary Slot" (Symbol). Place the Function in the slot, pull the lever to run it, then remove the Function from the slot.
Draw a Filing Cabinet (the Map). Each drawer is labeled with an event name. Inside each drawer is a list of phone numbers (callbacks). When an event happens, call all numbers in that drawer.
Draw an array wrapped in a "Magic Box". When the box is put next to a plus sign, a number pops out. When put next to a string, a string pops out.

Problem Title: Minimum String Length After Removing Substrings
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a vertical stack. As characters arrive, check if they "cancel out" the top item. If yes, pop. If no, push. The final stack size is the answer.
Draw the string. Place fingers at both ends. Move them toward the center. At each step, change the "bigger" letter to match the "smaller" letter.

Problem Title: Find the Punishment Number of an Integer
How to Draw (Opt): Write the number (e.g., 1296). Draw lines splitting it: 1|296, 12|96, 1|2|9|6, etc. If any branch sums to the target, it's a valid punishment number.
Draw the graph. Path A is fixed. Path B has "stretchy" edges (-1). Stretch the -1 edges until the total length of Path B matches the target distance exactly.
Draw two matching tree structures (Objects/Arrays). Trace down both simultaneously with two fingers. If a leaf node differs, circle it and write `[val1, val2]`.

Optimised Pattern: Row-by-Row Memory Copy (Intermediate tables filling up Cartesian space).
How to Draw (Opt): Plot transactions on a horizontal timeline grouped by customer. Highlight consecutive days with increasing amounts as unified blocks (islands).

Problem Title: Minimum Operations to Make Numbers Non-positive
Optimised Pattern: Array modification tracking (snapshotting the entire array state at each simulated step).
How to Draw (Opt): Pick a midpoint mid . Map elements to the number of extra X operations needed. If total extra ops ≤ mid , go left; else go right.

Optimised Pattern: Call Stack & Arguments Object frame allocation.
How to Draw (Opt): Visualize args as an array object containing a metadata header. Read the .length property directly without looping.

Problem Title: To Be Or Not To Be (JS)
Optimised Pattern: Heap memory allocation map (Drawing separate object allocations in memory for every closure context created).
How to Draw (Opt): Pass val into the factory block. Show the block emitting an object with two buttons: toBe and notToBe . Pushing a button feeds val2 into a strict equality === gate with the stored val .

Optimised Pattern: Call Stack & Redundant Object Allocation (Draw stacked boxes for deep recursion + identical clone structures in heap).
How to Draw (Opt): Start at the root JSON node. If a branch leads to a falsy value (0, false, null), visually cut the branch. If it leads to an array/object, traverse deeper before filtering.

Optimised Pattern: Loop pointers footprint (Draw i and j pointers constantly jumping back and forth across an array).
How to Draw (Opt): Slide a pointer across the array once. Maintain two buckets: min1 and min2 . When you find a smaller number, shift min1 into min2 , and update min1 .

Optimised Pattern: Deep Call Stack Grid (Showing massive stack frames for O(N) depth and O(2^N) repeated states).
How to Draw (Opt): Create a DP array of size N+1. Iterate through the string. At each index, either skip 1 char (dp[i+1] = dp[i]+1) or check if a dictionary word matches starting here, and "jump" forward to update that index's minimum cost.

Optimised Pattern: Exponential State Tracking (Visualizing memory holding all subsets simultaneously or deep recursion tree).
How to Draw (Opt): Separate numbers into Positives, Negatives, and Zeros. Multiply all positives. Sort negatives; multiply them in pairs. If an odd number of negatives exists, discard the one closest to zero. Ignore zeros (unless all zeros/one negative).

Optimised Pattern: Massive NxN Grid Matrix (Highlighting the memory waste of storing every direct edge).
How to Draw (Opt): Factorize each number. Instead of linking numbers to numbers, link numbers to their Prime Factors using DSU. If two numbers share a prime factor, they naturally fall into the same connected component. Check if the DSU has only 1 component left.

Problem Title: Remove Trailing Zeros From a String
Optimised Pattern: Heap Allocation Overload (Visualize multiple intermediate string objects piling up in memory due to immutable string slicing).
How to Draw (Opt): Place a pointer at the rightmost edge. Move left until you hit a non-zero character. Mark that index, then take one final slice from index 0 to that mark.

Problem Title: Difference of Number of Distinct Values on Diagonals
Optimised Pattern: Disposable HashSets (Draw hundreds of temporary Sets being created and garbage-collected for every individual cell).
How to Draw (Opt): Isolate each diagonal line. Run down the diagonal keeping a "running Set" of seen values (prefix). Then run back up keeping a running Set of the other side (suffix). Update answers in place.

Problem Title: Minimum Cost to Make All Characters Equal
Optimised Pattern: Deep Recursion Stack (Visualizing maximum stack depth O(N) but an exploding number of string copies across states).
How to Draw (Opt): Scan left to right. When s[i] != s[i-1] , you MUST flip something to unify them. You greedily choose the cheaper flip: either flip everything to the left (cost i ) or everything to the right (cost n-i ). Add min(i, n-i) to total cost.

Problem Title: Maximum Strictly Increasing Cells in a Matrix
Optimised Pattern: Unbounded Call Stack (Deep recursion for every valid path permutation).
How to Draw (Opt): Group cells by value and sort ascending. To calculate the longest path ending at value X at (i,j), look at the best previous results recorded in rowMax[i] and colMax[j] . Update answers simultaneously for identical values.

Problem Title: Find Shortest Path with K Hops
Optimised Pattern: Brute Force Path Memory (Storing every conceivable route configuration simultaneously).
How to Draw (Opt): Treat the graph as having K+1 parallel "floors". Taking a normal edge keeps you on the same floor. Taking a hop edge drops you down to the next floor with 0 cost. Run standard Dijkstra to find the shortest path to the destination on any floor.

Optimised Pattern: Floating Closure Memory (The callback and its lexical environment stay in memory until the timer naturally finishes).
How to Draw (Opt): Start the timer and capture its ID. Return a cancel function. If the cancel function is invoked before the delay, immediately pass the ID to clearTimeout , destroying the timer instantly.

Optimised Pattern: Massive String Reallocations (Immutable strings mean creating a brand new string in memory for every deletion).
How to Draw (Opt): Realize the problem effectively just asks for the number of unique characters in the string, since any duplicates can be targeted and destroyed. Dump characters into a Set and return its size.

Optimised Pattern: In-place array mutations (Unnecessary write operations to memory).
How to Draw (Opt): Find index of 1 (`idx1`) and N (`idxN`). Steps for 1 = `idx1`. Steps for N = `Length - 1 - idxN`. Total = `idx1 + steps_for_N`. If 1 started to the right of N (`idx1 > idxN`), they cross paths during swapping, saving 1 step, so subtract 1.

Optimised Pattern: O(N^2) 2D Array Allocation (Massive memory waste for large N).
How to Draw (Opt): Process queries from last to first. The last query applied to a row/col is the one that survives. Maintain a count of available rows/cols. If processing a new row, add `val * available_cols` to total, and mark row as seen. Decrement available rows.

Optimised Pattern: Trillions of temporary string allocations for digit extraction.
How to Draw (Opt): Build the number digit by digit (0-9). Track `current_sum`. If `is_tight` is true, the loop stops at the digit of the upper bound number. If `current_sum > max_sum`, prune the branch immediately. Cache states `(index, current_sum, is_tight)`.
Draw a directed graph. Flip it so arrows go both ways. List every user and simply count the arrows touching them. Divide by total nodes.

Optimised Pattern: Blocking Execution Context (One task occupies the stack while others wait in the queue).
How to Draw (Opt): Start all functions immediately. Maintain a resolvedCount and a results array. Each time a promise resolves, place the result at its specific index and increment the count. If count equals the total, resolve the main promise.

Optimised Pattern: Temporary Object Accumulation (Creating massive amounts of intermediate merged objects).
How to Draw (Opt): Create a single Map. Iterate through `arr1` and put every object into the Map using `id` as the key. Iterate through `arr2`; if an ID exists, merge properties into the existing object. Sort keys at the end.

Optimised Pattern: Serialized Memory Wait (The closure for the addition waits twice).
How to Draw (Opt): Trigger Promise.all([promise1, promise2]) . This allows both to resolve in parallel. Destructure the resulting array [val1, val2] and return the sum.

Optimised Pattern: Repeated Function Execution Stack (Multiple redundant calls to the selector function).
How to Draw (Opt): Pass a comparator to the native sort: (a, b) => fn(a) - fn(b) . The engine uses O(N log N) comparisons, minimizing the calls to the selector function.

Optimised Pattern: Accumulating Closure Scopes (Each timeout creates a new closure, potentially keeping old variables in memory longer).
How to Draw (Opt): Call the function immediately. Then, start a setInterval . Return a function that calls clearInterval . This ensures the first run happens at t=0 and subsequent runs follow a strict heartbeat.

Optimised Pattern: Multiple Statement Contexts (Each call is a separate statement in the execution stack).
How to Draw (Opt): In every method (add, subtract, etc.), perform the math and then return this; . This allows the output of one method to be the context for the next.

Optimised Pattern: Intermediate Array Allocation (Visualize a potentially massive array of strings being created in memory just for a boolean check).
How to Draw (Opt): Start a for...in loop. If the loop executes even once, return false immediately. If the loop finishes without executing, return true .

Problem Title: Count Houses in a Circular Street
Optimised Pattern: Unbounded Integer Accumulator (Memory usage of the counter variable growing as it fails to terminate).
How to Draw (Opt): Loop 1000 times (max possible n) and close all doors. Then open the door at the current house. Walk and count until you hit an open door.

Problem Title: Check if The Number is Fascinating
Optimised Pattern: Temporary String Buffers (Visualizing multiple string concatenations and copies).
How to Draw (Opt): Concatenate n, 2n, and 3n into one string. If length isn't 9, return false. Use a boolean array of size 10 to mark seen digits. If a digit is '0' or seen twice, return false.

Optimised Pattern: String Slice Memory (Draw many temporary substring copies being created in the heap).
How to Draw (Opt): Expand `right` pointer. If `s[right] == s[right-1]`, increment a `duplicates` counter. While `duplicates > 1`, move `left` pointer forward; if `s[left] == s[left+1]`, decrement `duplicates`. Track max `right - left + 1`.

Optimised Pattern: Temporal State Snapshots (Visualizing the position of every robot at every tick of time).
How to Draw (Opt): Key Insight: Robots "passing through" each other is mathematically identical to bouncing. Calculate final positions: `pos + (dir * d)`. Sort them. Use Prefix Sums to calculate all-pairs distances in O(N).

Problem Title: Find a Good Subset of Matrix
Optimised Pattern: Combinatorial Row Storage (Visualizing all row combinations in memory).
How to Draw (Opt): Each row has only <= 5 columns. Convert each row to a bitmask (2^5 = 32 possible masks). If any row is 0, return it. Otherwise, check all pairs of existing masks. If `(maskA & maskB) == 0`, those two rows form a good subset.

Optimised Pattern: Sort Buffer (Visualize the O(N) or O(log N) extra space used by sorting).
How to Draw (Opt): If array length < 3, return -1. Otherwise, just take the first three elements. Sort only those three (constant time). Return the one that isn't the min or max.

Problem Title: Lexicographically Smallest String After Substring Operation
Optimised Pattern: Exponential String Allocation (Visualize the heap filling with modified versions of the string).
How to Draw (Opt): Find the first character that is NOT 'a'. Start the operation there. Continue the operation until you hit the next 'a' or the end of the string. If the string is all 'a's, flip only the very last 'a' to 'z'.

Optimised Pattern: Redundant Array Rotation Buffers (Storing the state of the array for every single shift).
How to Draw (Opt): Iterate k from 0 to N-1 (rotations). Maintain an array min_costs where min_costs[i] is the minimum price found for chocolate i across all rotations so far. Sum min_costs at each k and add the shifting cost k * x .

Optimised Pattern: Query/Pair Stack (Drawing the search range for every individual query).
How to Draw (Opt): Sort pairs by nums1 descending and queries by x descending. As you process a query, add all pairs that now satisfy nums1 ≥ x to a monotonic stack (tracking nums2 and sum ). Binary search the stack for the best y .

Optimised Pattern: Distance Array + Priority Queue (Drawing the standard Dijkstra memory footprint).
How to Draw (Opt): Since we only need the closest marked node, standard Dijkstra from the source is already quite efficient. To optimize for multiple starts, one could run a single Dijkstra starting with all marked nodes in the PQ (if the edges were reversed).

Optimised Pattern: Buffer Scan (Visualizing the database engine reading the text column repeatedly for each word).
How to Draw (Opt): Use a CASE statement or UNION ALL to check for " bull " and " bear " within spaces to avoid partial matches. Group by the word name and sum the counts.

Optimised Pattern: Integer State Mutation (Visualizing the variables main and additional changing over time).
How to Draw (Opt): Total distance is (mainTank + min((mainTank - 1) / 4, additionalTank)) * 10 . This formula accounts for the "bonus" liters earned every 5 liters used.

Problem Title: Find the Value of the Partition
Optimised Pattern: Partition Memory Sets (Visualizing two massive separate heaps for every single partition attempt).
How to Draw (Opt): Sort the array. The minimum difference between any max(nums1) and min(nums2) must occur between two adjacent elements in the sorted version. Iterate once and find min(nums[i] - nums[i-1]) .

Optimised Pattern: Stack Frame Explosion (Visualizing a call stack N-levels deep repeated billions of times).
How to Draw (Opt): Use a DP state dp(mask, last_index) . mask tracks used elements; last_index ensures the next element is "special" relative to the previous. Sum up recursive results with memoization.

Optimised Pattern: Redundant Decision Paths (Visualizing same wall counts being re-calculated in different branches).
How to Draw (Opt): Key insight: Buying time[i] + 1 walls of time allows the free painter to paint the rest. State: dp[rem_walls] is min cost to paint rem_walls . Update dp[j] = min(dp[j], dp[j - time[i] - 1] + cost[i]) .

Optimised Pattern: Heap Fragment Memory (Visualizing thousands of temporary string objects created during slicing).
How to Draw (Opt): Move right pointer. If s[right] is already in the window, move left until it's removed. For every right , the number of valid substrings ending there is right - left + 1 . Sum these.

Problem Title: Find Maximum Number of String Pairs
Optimised Pattern: Temporary Reversed String Buffers (Creating a reversed copy for every comparison).
How to Draw (Opt): Iterate through words . For each word, check if its reverse is already in a seen Set. If yes, increment count and remove from Set. Else, add the word to the Set.

Optimised Pattern: Recursive Call Stack (Visualizing depth-first search for the longest possible valid string path).
How to Draw (Opt): Observe that "AB" can always be placed next to itself. "AA" and "BB" must alternate. The longest sequence of "AA" and "BB" is 2 * min(x, y) + (x != y ? 1 : 0) . Add all z ("AB") blocks. Multiply total blocks by 2.

Optimised Pattern: Massive String Heap (Visualizing huge strings being copied and stored at every level of recursion).
How to Draw (Opt): Key insight: Only the first and last characters of the current string matter for future merges. State: dp[i][first][last] is the min length using words up to i with specific start/end chars. Transition by checking if word's edge matches string's edge.

Optimised Pattern: Linear Search Pointers (Drawing the search range for every individual query).
How to Draw (Opt): Sort logs by time and queries by time. Use two pointers ( left and right ) to maintain a window of logs within [queries[i]-x, queries[i]] . Use a frequency map to track the number of active servers in the window.

Optimised Pattern: Repeated Digit Extraction (Drawing the logic to pull digits from numbers inside the nested loops).
How to Draw (Opt): Iterate through nums . For the current nums[j] , its last digit can pair with the first digits of all nums[i] seen so far. Use a frequency array of size 10 to store counts of "first digits" seen. Check GCD(1..9, last_digit) against the frequency array.

Problem Title: Minimum Operations to Make the Integer Zero
Optimised Pattern: Queue Memory (Visualizing the BFS queue filling with millions of possible intermediate numbers).
How to Draw (Opt): Let k be the number of operations. Then num1 - k*num2 must be representable as the sum of k powers of 2. This is true if bitCount(num1 - k*num2) <= k and k <= (num1 - k*num2) . Iterate k from 1 to 100.

Problem Title: Ways to Split Array Into Good Subarrays
Optimised Pattern: Recursive Call Stack (Visualizing the depth-first search through all valid and invalid partition combinations).
How to Draw (Opt): Find indices of all '1's. The number of ways to split between two '1's at indices i and j is the number of zeros between them + 1 (j - i). Multiply all these "gap counts" together.

Optimised Pattern: State Array Snapshots (Drawing the entire array state for every microscopic time increment).
How to Draw (Opt): Sort robots by position. Use a stack to store robots moving 'R'. When a robot moving 'L' appears, pop 'R' robots from the stack one by one to collide. Update health accordingly. Only survivors remain. Sort back to original order.

Problem Title: Customers with Maximum Number of Transactions on Consecutive Days (SQL)
Optimised Pattern: Temporary Join Tables (Visualizing the SQL engine creating massive intermediate tables for each join).
How to Draw (Opt): Use ROW_NUMBER() OVER(PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY transaction_date) . Subtract this from the date. Consecutive dates will result in the same "base date" (the island). Group by this base date.

Problem Title: Count Houses in a Circular Street II
How to Draw (Opt): Close the door at the start. Move k houses (max limit), closing all doors as you go. Then open the door at the start. Walk until you hit that open door. The number of steps is the count.

Optimised Pattern: Temporary Property Allocation (Mutation of the target object).
How to Draw (Opt): Return a new function that uses originalFn.apply(context, args) . This leverages the engine's ability to switch the this binding without modifying the context object.

Optimised Pattern: Deep Call Stack (Visualize memory frames for every nested level of the JSON structure).
How to Draw (Opt): Traverse both objects. If both values are objects (and not arrays), recursively merge. If both are arrays, return obj2 . Handle null and primitives as leaf nodes where obj2 always wins.

Optimised Pattern: Network Socket Overload (Visualizing multiple open connections consuming system file descriptors).
How to Draw (Opt): When a call comes in, push it to a pending queue. Use a Promise.resolve().then() or setTimeout(0) to schedule a single flush that processes all accumulated queries in one batch call.

Optimised Pattern: Iterative Pointer Mutation (Visualizing the CPU cycle overhead for millions of small increments).
How to Draw (Opt): Calculate the new index directly using (current + steps % n + n) % n . This handles large positive and negative steps instantly in one operation.

Optimised Pattern: Multiple Boolean Flags (Visualizing all the manual state variables needed for date math).
How to Draw (Opt): Get the current date time, add 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000 milliseconds, and create a new Date object. The built-in engine handles all roll-overs and leap years automatically.
Draw the string. Draw a "Consumer" pac-man. It eats a `{`, opens a new empty Object box, and calls a mini-pac-man to eat the keys/values until it hits `}`.

Problem Title: Longest Even Odd Subarray With Threshold
Optimised Pattern: Intermediate Subarray Slices (Visualizing the heap filling with transient array segments).
How to Draw (Opt): Scan the array. When you find an even number <= threshold, start a window. Expand while nums[i] <= threshold and nums[i] % 2 != nums[i-1] % 2 . Update max length and jump the start pointer to the end of the failed condition.
Draw a grid of numbers 1 to N. Cross out multiples of 2, 3, 5. Then, put a left finger on 2 and right finger on N-2. If both fingers are on uncrossed numbers, record them. Slide fingers inward.

Optimised Pattern: Redundant Min/Max Scans (Drawing a search arrow within every window).
How to Draw (Opt): Use a sliding window. Maintain a data structure (like two Deques or a TreeMap) to keep track of the current min and max in O(1) or O(log K). If max - min > 2 , shrink the window from the left.

Problem Title: Sum of Imbalance Numbers of All Subarrays
Optimised Pattern: Massive Sort Buffer Allocation (Drawing the memory used to sort N^2 subarrays).
How to Draw (Opt): Iterate with a fixed start and move end right. Keep a Set of numbers seen. When adding x, an imbalance decreases if x-1 or x+1 was already there, but increases if it's a "new" gap. Maintain a running current_imbalance .

Problem Title: Is Array a Preorder of Some Binary Tree
How to Draw (Opt): Draw a vertical tube (stack). Push the root. Read the next node. If its depth is deeper, push it. If it's shallower, pop the tube until you find its parent depth. If the parent doesn't exist, it's invalid.

Optimised Pattern: Subarray Buffer (Visualizing the heap filling with transient slices of the input array).
How to Draw (Opt): Iterate through the array. If a pair (i, i+1) has a difference of 1, start a window. Expand as long as the difference alternates. When it breaks, if the current difference is 1, restart from the previous element; otherwise, look for the next 1.

Optimised Pattern: Linear Memory Scan (Visualizing the CPU reading the entire marbles array for every single instruction).
How to Draw (Opt): Store initial positions in a Set. For each move, remove the from position and add the to position. The Set handles the "all marbles at this position" rule automatically. Sort the final Set keys.

Problem Title: Partition String Into Minimum Beautiful Substrings
How to Draw (Opt): Draw the binary string. Below it, draw a DP array. From index `i`, draw backward arcs to previous valid split points `j`. Write the minimum arc path length in `dp[i]`.

Optimised Pattern: O(M· N) Matrix Allocation (Massive memory waste for grids like 10^5 x 10^5).
How to Draw (Opt): A black cell at (r, c) affects up to four 2x2 blocks. Use a HashMap to store the count of black cells for each 2x2 block, identified by its top-left corner. Iterate only over the provided black cells. Calculate the "zero-black-cell" count by subtracting found blocks from the total.

Optimised Pattern: BFS Queue (Visualizing the search space widening with every time step).
How to Draw (Opt): In each of the t steps, you can increase num by 1 and decrease x by 1 to bring them closer. To maximize x , assume x starts far above num . They meet in the middle after 2 * t total distance relative to each other. Result: num + 2 * t .

Problem Title: Maximum Number of Jumps to Reach the Last Index
Optimised Pattern: Recursive Call Stack (Visualizing deep paths and redundant calculations of the same sub-problems).
How to Draw (Opt): Let dp[i] be the max jumps to reach index i . For each i , iterate through all j < i . If dp[j] != -1 and the jump condition is met, dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1) .

Problem Title: Longest Non-decreasing Subarray From Two Arrays
Optimised Pattern: Exponential State Memory (Visualizing the storage of all valid subarray combinations).
How to Draw (Opt): Maintain dp1[i] (max length ending at i using nums1[i] ) and dp2[i] (using nums2[i] ). Transition by checking nums1[i] against nums1[i-1] and nums2[i-1] . O(N).

Problem Title: Apply Operations to Make All Array Elements Zero
Optimised Pattern: In-place Array Mutations (Visualizing the CPU repeatedly writing to memory for every range update).
How to Draw (Opt): Keep track of a current_reduction . As you move right, subtract the reduction that "expires" (from i-k ). The value needed to zero nums[i] is nums[i] - current_reduction . If positive, add it to the reduction.

Optimised Pattern: Visited Node HashSet (Visualizing extra memory to store every node ID to prevent infinite loops).
How to Draw (Opt): Key insight: A node is a "special leaf" if its left child's right is itself, or similar. In DFS, if a node is a leaf, return 0. Otherwise, return 1 + max(leftHeight, rightHeight) . No visited set needed if logic is leaf-specific.
Use low and high pointers. While low <= high , check mid . If arr[mid] <= target , move low to mid + 1 . The final answer is the last valid index found.

Optimised Pattern: Recursive Call Stack (Visualize memory frames for every nested level of the object).
How to Draw (Opt): Use a recursive function that returns the transformed value. For arrays, use map() . For objects, use reduce() or Object.entries() . If value is undefined , return null .

Optimised Pattern: Blocking Main Thread (Visualizing the CPU stuck in a loop waiting for the async task).
How to Draw (Opt): Return a new function that returns new Promise((resolve, reject) => { ... }) . Inside, call the original function, passing a custom callback that resolves or rejects the promise.

Optimised Pattern: Heap Array Allocation (Visualizing thousands of string objects being stored simultaneously).
How to Draw (Opt): Use function* . In a while loop, yield the current date string, then increment the date by one day. This only computes the next date when requested.

Problem Title: Sum of Squares of Special Elements
Optimised Pattern: O(1) State Variables (Draw one sum and one i ).
How to Draw (Opt): Instead of checking all numbers up to N, find all divisors of N by iterating only up to √N . For each divisor d , both d and N/d are indices to check.

Problem Title: Maximum Beauty of an Array After Applying Operation
Optimised Pattern: Frequency Map over Range (Visualizing a large array tracking every possible integer value).
How to Draw (Opt): Sort the array. Find the longest subarray where max - min <= 2k . Use two pointers: expand right , and if the condition breaks, move left .

Problem Title: Minimum Index of a Valid Split
Optimised Pattern: Dual Frequency Maps (Visualizing two HashMaps being fully rebuilt for every possible split point).
How to Draw (Opt): First, find the dominant element of the whole array (only one can exist). Then, iterate once more, keeping a running count x of that element. At index i , check if x * 2 > i + 1 and (totalCount - x) * 2 > (n - i - 1) .

Problem Title: Length of the Longest Valid Substring
Optimised Pattern: Redundant String Slicing (Visualizing thousands of temporary substring copies being checked against a HashSet).
How to Draw (Opt): Move right pointer. For the new character at right , check only the last 10 characters (since forbidden strings length <= 10). If a forbidden word is found at right - k , update left to right - k + 1 .

Optimised Pattern: Minimal Memory (Only tracking unique category representatives).
How to Draw (Opt): Maintain a list of "Representative" items (one for each category found so far). For a new item, check it against the representatives. If no match, add it to the representatives.

Optimised Pattern: Large Intermediate Join (Visualizing the DB loading every single booking record into memory before counting).
How to Draw (Opt): LEFT JOIN Bookings to Flights. GROUP BY flight_id . Calculate booked_cnt as LEAST(COUNT(booking_id), capacity) and waitlist_cnt as GREATEST(COUNT(booking_id) - capacity, 0) .

Optimised Pattern: Sort Buffer (Visualizing the O(N) or O(log N) extra space used by the sorting algorithm).
How to Draw (Opt): If arr.length != n (where n = max + 1), return false. Use a frequency array. Every number 1 dots n-2 must appear once, and n-1 must appear twice.

Optimised Pattern: String Mutation Buffer (Visualizing repeated string recreations for each swap in languages with immutable strings).
How to Draw (Opt): Iterate through the string to extract all vowels into a list. Sort the list of vowels. Iterate through the original string again; whenever you hit a vowel position, replace it with the next vowel from the sorted list.

Problem Title: Visit Array Positions to Maximize Score
Optimised Pattern: Recursive Call Stack (Visualizing the massive overhead of re-calculating the best path for the same index multiple times).
How to Draw (Opt): Maintain two variables: even_max and odd_max (the best scores ending on an even or odd number so far). For the current number, if it's even, its best score is nums[i] + max(even_max, odd_max - x) . Update the parity maxes.

Problem Title: Ways to Express an Integer as Sum of Powers
Optimised Pattern: Exponential State Search (Visualizing all possible combinations of powers being summed).
How to Draw (Opt): Create a DP array dp where dp[i] is the number of ways to sum to i . For each number v = j^p , iterate the DP array backwards: dp[i] = (dp[i] + dp[i - v]) % mod .

Optimised Pattern: Repeated String Concatenation (Visualizing memory churn from adding one char at a time to a string).
How to Draw (Opt): For each string, call s.split(separator) . Flatten the results into one list. Filter out any empty strings "" produced by adjacent separators or leading/trailing separators.

Problem Title: Largest Element in an Array after Merge Operations
Optimised Pattern: Intermediate Array Reallocations (Visualizing the heap creating new arrays for every merge pass).
How to Draw (Opt): Start from the last element. Move left. If nums[i] <= current_sum , add nums[i] to current_sum (snowball effect). If not, current_sum resets to nums[i] . The answer is the max current_sum found.

Problem Title: Maximum Number of Groups With Increasing Lengths
Optimised Pattern: Priority Queue Heap State (Visualizing the heap re-balancing for every element used in a group).
How to Draw (Opt): Sort usageLimits . Maintain a total sum of available slots. A group of size k+1 can be formed if the total available elements is >= frac(k+1)(k+2)2.

Problem Title: Count Paths That Can Form a Palindrome in a Tree
Optimised Pattern: Path Frequency Maps (Visualizing thousands of HashMaps created to check character counts for every path).
How to Draw (Opt): Represent characters 'a'-'z' as bits in a 26-bit integer. Perform DFS to calculate the cumulative XOR mask from root to each node. A path between u and v is palindromic if mask(u) ^ mask(v) has 0 or 1 bits set. Use a HashMap to count mask frequencies.

Problem Title: Count Nodes That Are Great Ancestors
Optimised Pattern: Redundant Traversal Stack (Visualizing the CPU re-visiting the same bottom nodes for every ancestor).
How to Draw (Opt): Perform a DFS. When entering a node, query a Fenwick Tree for the count of values already present that are smaller than node.val . Add node.val to the Fenwick Tree. Recurse. When leaving, subtract the entry count from the current count to get the subtree-specific total.

Optimised Pattern: Row-by-Row Comparison Buffer (Visualizing the SQL engine re-scanning the bookings table for every single record).
How to Draw (Opt): Use ROW_NUMBER() OVER(PARTITION BY flight_id ORDER BY booking_time) as rn . A ticket is 'Confirmed' if rn <= capacity , otherwise 'Waitlisted' .

Optimised Pattern: Linear Search Overhead (Visualizing indexOf being called repeatedly).
How to Draw (Opt): Iterate through keysArr once using index i . Use obj[keysArr[i]] = valuesArr[i] . If the key already exists in the object (using in operator), skip it to maintain the first-occurrence rule.

Problem Title: Parallel Execution of Promises for Individual Results Retrieval
How to Draw (Opt): Draw 3 runners starting at the exact same time. Draw a "Finish Line Clerk" with a clipboard. As each runner crosses (success or fail), the clerk writes it down. When 3 names are written, the clerk hands over the clipboard.

Optimised Pattern: Heap Allocation Churn (Visualizing the heap filling with intermediate string fragments: "a", "aa", "aaa"...).
How to Draw (Opt): Native repeat(n) uses bitwise doubling: "a" -> "aa" -> "aaaa". Alternatively, new Array(n + 1).join(s) creates the final size once and fills it, avoiding intermediate copies.

Optimised Pattern: Argument Object Cloning (Visualizing the memory used to copy the arguments list for every call).
How to Draw (Opt): Return a function that merges args (fixed) and restArgs (dynamic). Iterate through args ; if it's the placeholder, take the next element from restArgs and increment a pointer. Use ...rest to catch extras.

Problem Title: Number of Employees Who Met the Target
Optimised Pattern: Sort Buffer (Visualizing the O(N) memory used to rearrange the array).
How to Draw (Opt): Iterate through the array once. Maintain a counter. If hours[i] >= target , increment the counter. Return count.

Problem Title: Count Complete Subarrays in an Array
Optimised Pattern: Massive Set Allocation (Visualizing thousands of HashSets being created and destroyed in memory).
How to Draw (Opt): Find total unique K. Use two pointers. Move right until the window contains K unique elements. Once it does, all subarrays starting at left and ending at or after right are "Complete" (there are n - right such subarrays). Move left and repeat.

Problem Title: Shortest String That Contains Three Strings
Optimised Pattern: String Concatenation Buffer (Visualizing the creation of 6 massive new strings in the heap).
How to Draw (Opt): For each of the 6 permutations, merge strings by checking if one is a substring of the other, or by finding the maximum overlap between the suffix of the first and prefix of the second. Track the smallest resulting string lexicographically.

Optimised Pattern: Trillions of Digit Arrays (Visualizing the CPU bottleneck from converting numbers to strings repeatedly).
How to Draw (Opt): Calculate count(high) - count(low-1) . Use a DP state: (index, last_digit, is_less, is_started) . Place digits 0-9 while ensuring they differ by 1 from last_digit and stay within the upper bound.

Optimised Pattern: Queue Memory (Visualizing the BFS queue growing linearly with K, potentially millions of strings).

Optimised Pattern: Static Array Memory (Visualizing a large block of heap memory occupied by fixed integers).
How to Draw (Opt): Use function* factorial() . Initialize res = 1 and i = 1 . Inside a while(true) loop, yield res , then update res *= ++i . This computes the next value only on demand.

Optimised Pattern: Execution Context Overhead (Visualizing the engine creating and destroying complex scopes for every iteration).

Draw a circle. Label the top "Execute Callback". Draw an arrow to the bottom labeled "Wait Delay". Draw an arrow back to the top. This forms a controlled loop.
